texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: CONCURRENT COMPOSITION FOR DIFFERENTIALLY PRIVATE CONTINUAL MECHANISMS
texts.text: CONCURRENT COMPOSITION FOR DIFFERENTIALLY PRIVATE CONTINUAL MECHANISMS

texts.label: text
texts.orig: MONIKA HENZINGER 1 , ROODABEH SAFAVI 2 , SALIL VADHAN 3
texts.text: MONIKA HENZINGER 1 , ROODABEH SAFAVI 2 , SALIL VADHAN 3

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Abstract. Many intended uses of differential privacy involve a continual mechanism that is set up to run continuously over a long period of time, making more statistical releases as either queries come in or the dataset is updated. In this paper, we give the first general treatment of privacy against adaptive adversaries for mechanisms that support dataset updates and a variety of queries, all arbitrarily interleaved. It also models a very general notion of neighboring, that includes both event-level and user-level privacy.
texts.text: Abstract. Many intended uses of differential privacy involve a continual mechanism that is set up to run continuously over a long period of time, making more statistical releases as either queries come in or the dataset is updated. In this paper, we give the first general treatment of privacy against adaptive adversaries for mechanisms that support dataset updates and a variety of queries, all arbitrarily interleaved. It also models a very general notion of neighboring, that includes both event-level and user-level privacy.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We prove several concurrent composition theorems for continual mechanisms, which ensure privacy even when an adversary can interleave its queries and dataset updates to the different composed mechanisms. Previous concurrent composition theorems for differential privacy were only for the case when the dataset is static, with no adaptive updates. Moreover, we also give the first interactive and continual generalizations of the 'parallel composition theorem' for noninteractive differential privacy. Specifically, we show that the analogue of the noninteractive parallel composition theorem holds if either there are no adaptive dataset updates or each of the composed mechanisms satisfies pure differential privacy, but it fails to hold for composing approximately differentially private mechanisms with dataset updates.
texts.text: We prove several concurrent composition theorems for continual mechanisms, which ensure privacy even when an adversary can interleave its queries and dataset updates to the different composed mechanisms. Previous concurrent composition theorems for differential privacy were only for the case when the dataset is static, with no adaptive updates. Moreover, we also give the first interactive and continual generalizations of the 'parallel composition theorem' for noninteractive differential privacy. Specifically, we show that the analogue of the noninteractive parallel composition theorem holds if either there are no adaptive dataset updates or each of the composed mechanisms satisfies pure differential privacy, but it fails to hold for composing approximately differentially private mechanisms with dataset updates.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We then formalize a set of general conditions on a continual mechanism M that runs multiple continual sub-mechanisms such that the privacy guarantees of M follow directly using the above concurrent composition theorems on the sub-mechanisms, without further privacy loss. This enables us to give a simpler and more modular privacy analysis of a recent continual histogram mechanism of Henzinger, Sricharan, and Steiner. In the case of approximate DP, ours is the first proof that shows that its privacy holds against adaptive adversaries.
texts.text: We then formalize a set of general conditions on a continual mechanism M that runs multiple continual sub-mechanisms such that the privacy guarantees of M follow directly using the above concurrent composition theorems on the sub-mechanisms, without further privacy loss. This enables us to give a simpler and more modular privacy analysis of a recent continual histogram mechanism of Henzinger, Sricharan, and Steiner. In the case of approximate DP, ours is the first proof that shows that its privacy holds against adaptive adversaries.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1. Introduction
texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Differential privacy [6] is the now-standard framework for protecting privacy when performing statistical analysis or machine learning on sensitive datasets about individuals. In addition to having a rich and rapidly growing scientific literature, differential privacy has also seen large-scale adoption by technology companies and government agencies.
texts.text: Differential privacy [6] is the now-standard framework for protecting privacy when performing statistical analysis or machine learning on sensitive datasets about individuals. In addition to having a rich and rapidly growing scientific literature, differential privacy has also seen large-scale adoption by technology companies and government agencies.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The familiar formulation of differential privacy is for a noninteractive mechanism M : X → Y , which takes a dataset x ∈ X and produces a statistical release (e.g. a collection of statistics or the parameters of a machine learning model) y ∈ Y . Differential privacy requires that every two datasets x and x ′ that 'differ on one individual's data,' the output distributions M ( x ) and M ( x ′ ) are 'close' to each other. Formally, we have a neighboring relation ∼ on X , which specifies which datasets 'differ on one individual's data,' or more generally what information requires privacy protection. We say that M is ( ϵ, δ ) -DP with respect to ∼ if for all x ∼ x ′ , we have:
texts.text: The familiar formulation of differential privacy is for a noninteractive mechanism M : X → Y , which takes a dataset x ∈ X and produces a statistical release (e.g. a collection of statistics or the parameters of a machine learning model) y ∈ Y . Differential privacy requires that every two datasets x and x ′ that 'differ on one individual's data,' the output distributions M ( x ) and M ( x ′ ) are 'close' to each other. Formally, we have a neighboring relation ∼ on X , which specifies which datasets 'differ on one individual's data,' or more generally what information requires privacy protection. We say that M is ( ϵ, δ ) -DP with respect to ∼ if for all x ∼ x ′ , we have:

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: (1) ∀ S ⊆ Y Pr[ M ( x ) ∈ S ] ≤ e ϵ · Pr[ M ( x ′ ) ∈ S ] + δ.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Typically we think of ϵ as a small constant, e.g. ϵ = . 1, while δ is cryptographically negligible. The case that δ = 0 is referred to as pure differential privacy, and otherwise Inequality 1 is known as approximate differential privacy. This simple definition is very useful and easy to work with, but does not capture many scenarios in which we want to apply differential privacy.
texts.text: Typically we think of ϵ as a small constant, e.g. ϵ = . 1, while δ is cryptographically negligible. The case that δ = 0 is referred to as pure differential privacy, and otherwise Inequality 1 is known as approximate differential privacy. This simple definition is very useful and easy to work with, but does not capture many scenarios in which we want to apply differential privacy.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: 1.1. Differential Privacy Over Time. Many intended uses of differential privacy involve a system that is set up to run continuously over a long period of time, making more statistical releases as either queries come in or the dataset is updated, for example with people leaving and entering. (The latter case, with dataset updates, is known as differential privacy under continual observation , and was introduced in the concurrent works of Dwork, Naor, Pitassi, and Rothblum [7] and Chan, Shi, and Song [2].) These settings introduce a new attack surface, where an adversary may be adaptive , issuing queries or influencing dataset updates based on the responses it receives.
texts.text: 1.1. Differential Privacy Over Time. Many intended uses of differential privacy involve a system that is set up to run continuously over a long period of time, making more statistical releases as either queries come in or the dataset is updated, for example with people leaving and entering. (The latter case, with dataset updates, is known as differential privacy under continual observation , and was introduced in the concurrent works of Dwork, Naor, Pitassi, and Rothblum [7] and Chan, Shi, and Song [2].) These settings introduce a new attack surface, where an adversary may be adaptive , issuing queries or influencing dataset updates based on the responses it receives.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: When δ > 0, there are mechanisms that are differentially private against oblivious adversaries but not against adaptive ones. 1 One natural example is the 'advanced composition theorem' for differentially privacy [9], which is known to fail if the adversary can select the privacy-loss parameters ( ϵ i , δ i ) of the composed mechanisms adaptively [19].
texts.text: When δ > 0, there are mechanisms that are differentially private against oblivious adversaries but not against adaptive ones. 1 One natural example is the 'advanced composition theorem' for differentially privacy [9], which is known to fail if the adversary can select the privacy-loss parameters ( ϵ i , δ i ) of the composed mechanisms adaptively [19].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Until recently, adaptivity has been treated in an ad hoc manner in the differential privacy literature. For example, the aforementioned advanced composition theorem of [9] was formalized in a way that allows an adversary to adaptively choose the mechanisms and pairs of adjacent datasets, just not the privacy-loss parameters, and new composition theorems that allow for adaptive choices of privacy-loss parameters were given by [19]. Other early examples where adaptive queries were analyzed include the Sparse Vector Technique (SVT) [8, 11, 17] and the Private Multiplicative Weights algorithm [11].
texts.text: Until recently, adaptivity has been treated in an ad hoc manner in the differential privacy literature. For example, the aforementioned advanced composition theorem of [9] was formalized in a way that allows an adversary to adaptively choose the mechanisms and pairs of adjacent datasets, just not the privacy-loss parameters, and new composition theorems that allow for adaptive choices of privacy-loss parameters were given by [19]. Other early examples where adaptive queries were analyzed include the Sparse Vector Technique (SVT) [8, 11, 17] and the Private Multiplicative Weights algorithm [11].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A few years ago, Vadhan and Wang [20] initiated a systematic study of differentially private mechanisms M that support adaptive queries. Specifically, they formalized the notion of an interactive mechanism , which begins with a dataset as input and maintains a potentially secret state as it answers queries that come in over time. (Both SVT and Private Multiplicative Weights utilize a secret state.) They defined M to be an ( ϵ, δ ) -DP interactive mechanism if for every pair of adjacent datasets x ∼ x ′ and every adaptive adversary A , the view of A when interacting with M ( x ) is ( ϵ, δ )-indistinguishable (in the sense of Inequality (1)) from its view when interacting with M ( x ′ ), where the view of A consists of the random coin tosses of A and the transcript of the interaction.
texts.text: A few years ago, Vadhan and Wang [20] initiated a systematic study of differentially private mechanisms M that support adaptive queries. Specifically, they formalized the notion of an interactive mechanism , which begins with a dataset as input and maintains a potentially secret state as it answers queries that come in over time. (Both SVT and Private Multiplicative Weights utilize a secret state.) They defined M to be an ( ϵ, δ ) -DP interactive mechanism if for every pair of adjacent datasets x ∼ x ′ and every adaptive adversary A , the view of A when interacting with M ( x ) is ( ϵ, δ )-indistinguishable (in the sense of Inequality (1)) from its view when interacting with M ( x ′ ), where the view of A consists of the random coin tosses of A and the transcript of the interaction.

texts.label: text

texts.label: page_header
texts.orig: CONCURRENT COMPOSITION FOR DIFFERENTIALLY PRIVATE CONTINUAL MECHANISMS
texts.text: CONCURRENT COMPOSITION FOR DIFFERENTIALLY PRIVATE CONTINUAL MECHANISMS

texts.label: text
texts.orig: composition of interactive mechanisms. In concurrent composition of interactive mechanisms, an adaptive adversary can interleave its queries to the different interactive mechanisms being composed. This is a realistic attack scenario when multiple interactive differentially private mechanisms are deployed in practice, and is also a useful primitive for analyzing differentially private algorithms that use several interactive mechanisms as building blocks. Vadhan and Wang showed that the basic composition theorem for pure differential privacy [6] does extend to concurrent composition, but for approximate differential privacy, they obtained a much weaker bound for the concurrent case. A tight bound for concurrent composition of approximate differential privacy was given by Lyu [18]. A series of works gave concurrent composition theorems for other flavors of differential privacy and composition, such as R´ enyi DP [18], f -DP [21], and adaptive choices of privacy parameters [10].
texts.text: composition of interactive mechanisms. In concurrent composition of interactive mechanisms, an adaptive adversary can interleave its queries to the different interactive mechanisms being composed. This is a realistic attack scenario when multiple interactive differentially private mechanisms are deployed in practice, and is also a useful primitive for analyzing differentially private algorithms that use several interactive mechanisms as building blocks. Vadhan and Wang showed that the basic composition theorem for pure differential privacy [6] does extend to concurrent composition, but for approximate differential privacy, they obtained a much weaker bound for the concurrent case. A tight bound for concurrent composition of approximate differential privacy was given by Lyu [18]. A series of works gave concurrent composition theorems for other flavors of differential privacy and composition, such as R´ enyi DP [18], f -DP [21], and adaptive choices of privacy parameters [10].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: All of the above works are for the case where the queries to the mechanisms are adaptive, but the dataset x is static, given as input to the mechanism at the start of the interaction. Jain, Raskhodnikova, Sivakumar, and Smith [13] recently gave a formalization of adaptive adversaries under continual observation (where there are dataset updates but the queries are fixed) for the special case of event-level privacy, where two streams of updates are considered adjacent if they differ on only one update. The lack of a more general formalism and toolkit for reasoning about adaptivity in dataset updates has led to complicated and ad hoc proofs of privacy of continual mechanisms (see e.g. [12]). Rectifying this situation is what motivated our work.
texts.text: All of the above works are for the case where the queries to the mechanisms are adaptive, but the dataset x is static, given as input to the mechanism at the start of the interaction. Jain, Raskhodnikova, Sivakumar, and Smith [13] recently gave a formalization of adaptive adversaries under continual observation (where there are dataset updates but the queries are fixed) for the special case of event-level privacy, where two streams of updates are considered adjacent if they differ on only one update. The lack of a more general formalism and toolkit for reasoning about adaptivity in dataset updates has led to complicated and ad hoc proofs of privacy of continual mechanisms (see e.g. [12]). Rectifying this situation is what motivated our work.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1.2. Our contributions. In this paper, we:
texts.text: 1.2. Our contributions. In this paper, we:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Give a formalism that captures privacy against adaptive adversaries for continual mechanisms , which support both queries and dataset updates. This generalizes the previously studied cases of interactive mechanisms (which have static datasets) and continual observation (which answer fixed queries). See Figure 1 for an illustration. We note that specific mechanisms, like variants of SVT, have been proposed to handle both dataset updates and queries [14], but without offering a definition of privacy against adversaries that can adaptively select both the updates and queries.
texts.text: Give a formalism that captures privacy against adaptive adversaries for continual mechanisms , which support both queries and dataset updates. This generalizes the previously studied cases of interactive mechanisms (which have static datasets) and continual observation (which answer fixed queries). See Figure 1 for an illustration. We note that specific mechanisms, like variants of SVT, have been proposed to handle both dataset updates and queries [14], but without offering a definition of privacy against adversaries that can adaptively select both the updates and queries.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · We prove concurrent composition theorems for continual mechanisms. (1) The first shows that, when the privacy-loss parameters are fixed, the standard composition theorems for noninteractive approximate differential privacy extend to the concurrent composition of continual mechanisms. (2) We extend the 'parallel composition theorem' for noninteractive mechanisms to the concurrent parallel composition of a fi nite or infinite number of adaptively chosen interactive mechanisms with an adaptively chosen pair of neighboring datasets. (3) Then we extend this further to the concurrent composition of a fi nite or infinite number of adaptively chosen continual mechanisms when the composed mechanisms satisfy pure differential privacy. (4) We complement the latter with a counterexample for the case of approximate differential privacy.
texts.text: We prove concurrent composition theorems for continual mechanisms. (1) The first shows that, when the privacy-loss parameters are fixed, the standard composition theorems for noninteractive approximate differential privacy extend to the concurrent composition of continual mechanisms. (2) We extend the 'parallel composition theorem' for noninteractive mechanisms to the concurrent parallel composition of a fi nite or infinite number of adaptively chosen interactive mechanisms with an adaptively chosen pair of neighboring datasets. (3) Then we extend this further to the concurrent composition of a fi nite or infinite number of adaptively chosen continual mechanisms when the composed mechanisms satisfy pure differential privacy. (4) We complement the latter with a counterexample for the case of approximate differential privacy.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · We formalize a set of general conditions on a continual mechanism M that runs multiple continual sub-mechanisms such that the privacy guarantees of M follow directly using the above concurrent composition theorems on the sub-mechanisms, without further privacy loss .
texts.text: We formalize a set of general conditions on a continual mechanism M that runs multiple continual sub-mechanisms such that the privacy guarantees of M follow directly using the above concurrent composition theorems on the sub-mechanisms, without further privacy loss .

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · We use our concurrent composition theorems for continual mechanisms, with the aforementioned general conditions, to give a simpler and more modular privacy analysis of a recent continual histogram mechanism [12]. In the case of approximate DP, ours is the first proof that the privacy of the mechanism holds against adaptive adversaries.
texts.text: We use our concurrent composition theorems for continual mechanisms, with the aforementioned general conditions, to give a simpler and more modular privacy analysis of a recent continual histogram mechanism [12]. In the case of approximate DP, ours is the first proof that the privacy of the mechanism holds against adaptive adversaries.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Towards defining privacy, we consider adaptive adversaries A that send messages m i for i = 1 , 2 , 3 , . . . that always are parsed as pairs m i = ( m i [0] , m i [1]). Privacy is defined with respect to a verification function f that takes a sequence of such pairs ( m 1 , m 2 , . . . , m t ) and outputs either ⊤ or ⊥ . When f ( m 1 , m 2 , . . . , m t ) = ⊤ , we consider the two sequences m [0] = ( m 1 [0] , m 2 [0] , . . . , m t [0]) and m [1] = ( m 1 [1] , m 2 [1] , . . . , m t [1]) to be neighboring .
texts.text: Towards defining privacy, we consider adaptive adversaries A that send messages m i for i = 1 , 2 , 3 , . . . that always are parsed as pairs m i = ( m i [0] , m i [1]). Privacy is defined with respect to a verification function f that takes a sequence of such pairs ( m 1 , m 2 , . . . , m t ) and outputs either ⊤ or ⊥ . When f ( m 1 , m 2 , . . . , m t ) = ⊤ , we consider the two sequences m [0] = ( m 1 [0] , m 2 [0] , . . . , m t [0]) and m [1] = ( m 1 [1] , m 2 [1] , . . . , m t [1]) to be neighboring .

texts.label: page_header
texts.orig: CONCURRENT COMPOSITION FOR DIFFERENTIALLY PRIVATE CONTINUAL MECHANISMS
texts.text: CONCURRENT COMPOSITION FOR DIFFERENTIALLY PRIVATE CONTINUAL MECHANISMS

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Now, by the choice of the verification function f , we can easily capture previously studied notions of privacy and more. For example, for a standard interactive mechanism, f ( m 1 , m 2 , . . . , m t ) will check that m 1 [0] ∼ m 1 [1] are a pair of neighboring datasets, and that m i [0] = m i [1] for all i > 1 (these represent the adaptive queries). For continual observation under event-level privacy, we interpret all of the m i [ b ]'s as dataset updates, and f will require that m [0] and m [1] differ in at most one coordinate i ∈ [ k ]. For continual observation under 'user-level' privacy, f will require that the entries in which m [0] and m [1] differ correspond to only one user. For continual mechanisms, we can interleave adaptive queries among the dataset updates by having f require that all m i [0] = m i [1] whenever either one is a query (rather than a dataset update).
texts.text: Now, by the choice of the verification function f , we can easily capture previously studied notions of privacy and more. For example, for a standard interactive mechanism, f ( m 1 , m 2 , . . . , m t ) will check that m 1 [0] ∼ m 1 [1] are a pair of neighboring datasets, and that m i [0] = m i [1] for all i > 1 (these represent the adaptive queries). For continual observation under event-level privacy, we interpret all of the m i [ b ]'s as dataset updates, and f will require that m [0] and m [1] differ in at most one coordinate i ∈ [ k ]. For continual observation under 'user-level' privacy, f will require that the entries in which m [0] and m [1] differ correspond to only one user. For continual mechanisms, we can interleave adaptive queries among the dataset updates by having f require that all m i [0] = m i [1] whenever either one is a query (rather than a dataset update).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To go from a verification function f to a definition of privacy against adaptive adversaries, we utilize the notion of an interactive post-processing mechanism (IPM) [10], which is a stateful procedure that can stand between an interactive mechanism and an adversary, or two interactive or continual mechanisms, or even two IPMs, and interact with both of them adaptively, sending and receiving messages to the mechanisms on its left and right. Specifically, we place two IPMs between the adversary A and the continual mechanism M . The first is a verifier V [ f ] that, every time it receives a new message pair m k from A , applies f to the entire history of message pairs ( m 1 , . . . , m k ) sent by A , and forwards m k onward only if f returns ⊤ . Otherwise V [ f ] halts the interaction. The second IPM is the identifier I , which starts out with a secret bit b ∈ { 0 , 1 } , and every time it receives a message pair ( m i [0] , m i [1]) from V [ f ], it forwards m i [ b ] on to the mechanism M . When M returns an answer a i , I and V [ f ] just forward it back to A . See Figure 2 for an illustration of an interaction between A and V [ f ] ◦ ∗ I [1] ◦ ∗ M consisting of three messages from A to V [ f ].
texts.text: To go from a verification function f to a definition of privacy against adaptive adversaries, we utilize the notion of an interactive post-processing mechanism (IPM) [10], which is a stateful procedure that can stand between an interactive mechanism and an adversary, or two interactive or continual mechanisms, or even two IPMs, and interact with both of them adaptively, sending and receiving messages to the mechanisms on its left and right. Specifically, we place two IPMs between the adversary A and the continual mechanism M . The first is a verifier V [ f ] that, every time it receives a new message pair m k from A , applies f to the entire history of message pairs ( m 1 , . . . , m k ) sent by A , and forwards m k onward only if f returns ⊤ . Otherwise V [ f ] halts the interaction. The second IPM is the identifier I , which starts out with a secret bit b ∈ { 0 , 1 } , and every time it receives a message pair ( m i [0] , m i [1]) from V [ f ], it forwards m i [ b ] on to the mechanism M . When M returns an answer a i , I and V [ f ] just forward it back to A . See Figure 2 for an illustration of an interaction between A and V [ f ] ◦ ∗ I [1] ◦ ∗ M consisting of three messages from A to V [ f ].

texts.orig: This leads to the following definition of differential privacy for continual mechanisms:
texts.text: This leads to the following definition of differential privacy for continual mechanisms:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Definition 1.1 (DP for continual mechanisms) . Let f be a verification function. A continual mechanism M is ( ϵ, δ )-DP with respect to f if for every adaptive adversary A , the view of A when interacting with V [ f ] ◦ ∗ I [0] ◦ ∗ M is ( ϵ, δ ) -indistinguishable from its view when interacting with V [ f ] ◦ ∗ I [1] ◦ ∗ M .
texts.text: Definition 1.1 (DP for continual mechanisms) . Let f be a verification function. A continual mechanism M is ( ϵ, δ )-DP with respect to f if for every adaptive adversary A , the view of A when interacting with V [ f ] ◦ ∗ I [0] ◦ ∗ M is ( ϵ, δ ) -indistinguishable from its view when interacting with V [ f ] ◦ ∗ I [1] ◦ ∗ M .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The idea of defining security against adaptive adversaries by having the adversary send pairs of messages and trying to distinguish whether the first message in each pair or the second message in each pair is being used goes back to the notion of left-or-right indistinguishability in crytography [1]. What is different here is its combination with a verification function f to enforce the neighboring condition, which is necessary to capture differential privacy.
texts.text: The idea of defining security against adaptive adversaries by having the adversary send pairs of messages and trying to distinguish whether the first message in each pair or the second message in each pair is being used goes back to the notion of left-or-right indistinguishability in crytography [1]. What is different here is its combination with a verification function f to enforce the neighboring condition, which is necessary to capture differential privacy.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As far as we know, this definition captures all of the previous definitions of privacy against adaptive adversaries as special cases (by appropriate choices of f ), as well as modeling adaptive adversaries in even more scenarios (such as continual observation with user-level privacy, and mixtures of adaptively-chosen dataset updates and queries).

texts.orig: Concurrent Composition Theorems for Continual Mechanisms. With the above definitions in hand, we can formulate and prove new concurrent composition theorems for continual mechanisms. Recall that with concurrent composition of interactive mechanisms, an adaptive adversary (or honest analyst) can interleave its queries to the different interactive mechanisms being composed; for concurrent composition of continual mechanisms, we also allow the dataset updates to be interleaved with the queries and each other. (See Figure 3.)
texts.text: Concurrent Composition Theorems for Continual Mechanisms. With the above definitions in hand, we can formulate and prove new concurrent composition theorems for continual mechanisms. Recall that with concurrent composition of interactive mechanisms, an adaptive adversary (or honest analyst) can interleave its queries to the different interactive mechanisms being composed; for concurrent composition of continual mechanisms, we also allow the dataset updates to be interleaved with the queries and each other. (See Figure 3.)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Our first concurrent composition theorem is as follows:
texts.text: Our first concurrent composition theorem is as follows:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Theorem 1.2 (concurrent composition of continual mechanisms, informally stated) . For every fixed sequence ( ϵ 1 , δ 1 ) , . . . , ( ϵ k , δ k ) of privacy-loss parameters and verification functions f 1 , . . . , f k , the concurrent composition of continual mechanisms M i that are ( ϵ i , δ i ) -DP wrt f i is ( ϵ, δ ) -DP for the same ( ϵ, δ ) that holds for composing noninteractive mechanisms that are ( ϵ i , δ i ) -DP.
texts.text: Theorem 1.2 (concurrent composition of continual mechanisms, informally stated) . For every fixed sequence ( ϵ 1 , δ 1 ) , . . . , ( ϵ k , δ k ) of privacy-loss parameters and verification functions f 1 , . . . , f k , the concurrent composition of continual mechanisms M i that are ( ϵ i , δ i ) -DP wrt f i is ( ϵ, δ ) -DP for the same ( ϵ, δ ) that holds for composing noninteractive mechanisms that are ( ϵ i , δ i ) -DP.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Notably, this result allows the adversary to adaptively and concurrently choose the neighboring sequences fed to each of the M i 's based on the responses received from the other mechanisms.
texts.text: Notably, this result allows the adversary to adaptively and concurrently choose the neighboring sequences fed to each of the M i 's based on the responses received from the other mechanisms.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Theorem 1.2 is proven by using the aforementioned reduction from continual to interactive mechanism. Then we use Lyu's result [18] for ( ϵ, δ )-DP interactive mechanisms and extend it to hold for infinite (but discrete) answer spaces and an unbounded number of rounds of interaction as follows. In Lyu's proof the restriction on the number of interactions comes from a backward definition of a so-called control function. We define a function which is the limit over this control function and, using monotone convergence, show that this 'limit function' satisfies all the necessary conditions to be used in the rest of Lyu's proof. This extends the result to an unbounded number of rounds of interaction. Furthermore, Lyu's proof used an algorithmic construction iterating over the answer set, implying that the answer set must be finite. Instead with a mathematical approach, we extend the proof to work with a countable, infinite answer set.
texts.text: Theorem 1.2 is proven by using the aforementioned reduction from continual to interactive mechanism. Then we use Lyu's result [18] for ( ϵ, δ )-DP interactive mechanisms and extend it to hold for infinite (but discrete) answer spaces and an unbounded number of rounds of interaction as follows. In Lyu's proof the restriction on the number of interactions comes from a backward definition of a so-called control function. We define a function which is the limit over this control function and, using monotone convergence, show that this 'limit function' satisfies all the necessary conditions to be used in the rest of Lyu's proof. This extends the result to an unbounded number of rounds of interaction. Furthermore, Lyu's proof used an algorithmic construction iterating over the answer set, implying that the answer set must be finite. Instead with a mathematical approach, we extend the proof to work with a countable, infinite answer set.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: While we focus on composing approximate DP with fixed privacy loss parameters ( ϵ i , δ i ) in Theorem 1.2, we expect that there should also be analogues of it for other settings in which concurrent composition of interactive mechanisms have been studied, such as R´ enyi DP [18], f -DP [21], and adaptively chosen privacy-loss parameters [10].

If M 1 , . . . , M ℓ are noninteractive mechanism, then it is known that M ′ satisfies the same level of ( ϵ, δ )-DP as the (standard) composition of k (rather than ℓ ) ( ϵ 0 , δ 0 )-DP mechanisms.

If M 1 , . . . , M ℓ are noninteractive mechanism, then it is known that M ′ satisfies the same level of ( ϵ, δ )-DP as the (standard) composition of k (rather than ℓ ) ( ϵ 0 , δ 0 )-DP mechanisms.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We study the extent to which parallel composition extends to the concurrent composition of continual mechanisms, meaning the neighboring relation is as above, but the adversary can interact concurrently with the mechanisms, and in particular decide adaptively on which k of them to have different update sequences. Moreover, we also consider the case where ℓ is unbounded, meaning the adversary (or honest analyst) can adaptively decide how many mechanisms to interact with. We refer to this as concurrent k -sparse parallel composition .
texts.text: We study the extent to which parallel composition extends to the concurrent composition of continual mechanisms, meaning the neighboring relation is as above, but the adversary can interact concurrently with the mechanisms, and in particular decide adaptively on which k of them to have different update sequences. Moreover, we also consider the case where ℓ is unbounded, meaning the adversary (or honest analyst) can adaptively decide how many mechanisms to interact with. We refer to this as concurrent k -sparse parallel composition .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: First, we extend the parallel composition of noninteractive mechanisms to concurrent composition of interactive mechanisms (where there are adaptive queries but datasets x i are static, rather than dynamically updated).
texts.text: First, we extend the parallel composition of noninteractive mechanisms to concurrent composition of interactive mechanisms (where there are adaptive queries but datasets x i are static, rather than dynamically updated).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Theorem 1.3 (concurrent parallel composition of interactive mechanisms) . For every ϵ 0 , δ 0 ≥ 0 and k ∈ N , and every ( ϵ 0 , δ 0 ) -DP interactive mechanism M 0 , the concurrent k -sparse parallel composition of an unbounded number of copies of M 0 satisfies the same ( ϵ, δ ) -DP guarantees as the composition of k noninteractive ( ϵ 0 , δ 0 ) -DP mechanisms.
texts.text: Theorem 1.3 (concurrent parallel composition of interactive mechanisms) . For every ϵ 0 , δ 0 ≥ 0 and k ∈ N , and every ( ϵ 0 , δ 0 ) -DP interactive mechanism M 0 , the concurrent k -sparse parallel composition of an unbounded number of copies of M 0 satisfies the same ( ϵ, δ ) -DP guarantees as the composition of k noninteractive ( ϵ 0 , δ 0 ) -DP mechanisms.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As discussed later in the introduction, we also prove a generalization of Theorem 1.3 for the case of composing interactive mechanisms M i with differing ( ϵ i , δ i ) parameters.
texts.text: As discussed later in the introduction, we also prove a generalization of Theorem 1.3 for the case of composing interactive mechanisms M i with differing ( ϵ i , δ i ) parameters.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Then we turn to the case of continual mechanisms , where there can also be adaptive dataset updates. We prove a negative result for approximate DP ( δ 0 > 0):
texts.text: Then we turn to the case of continual mechanisms , where there can also be adaptive dataset updates. We prove a negative result for approximate DP ( δ 0 > 0):

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Theorem 1.4 (concurrent parallel composition of approximate DP continual mechanisms, informally stated) . For every δ 0 > 0 , there is an (0 , δ 0 ) -DP continual mechanism M 0 such that for every ℓ ∈ N , the concurrent 1-sparse parallel composition of ℓ copies of M 0 is not (0 , δ ) -DP for any δ < 1 -(1 -δ 0 ) ℓ .
texts.text: Theorem 1.4 (concurrent parallel composition of approximate DP continual mechanisms, informally stated) . For every δ 0 > 0 , there is an (0 , δ 0 ) -DP continual mechanism M 0 such that for every ℓ ∈ N , the concurrent 1-sparse parallel composition of ℓ copies of M 0 is not (0 , δ ) -DP for any δ < 1 -(1 -δ 0 ) ℓ .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This theorem says that, at least in terms of the δ parameter, concurrent parallel composition of continual mechanisms does not provide any benefit over non-parallel composition, since the bound of 1 -(1 -δ 0 ) ℓ , is the bound that we would get by just applying composition over all ℓ mechanisms.
texts.text: This theorem says that, at least in terms of the δ parameter, concurrent parallel composition of continual mechanisms does not provide any benefit over non-parallel composition, since the bound of 1 -(1 -δ 0 ) ℓ , is the bound that we would get by just applying composition over all ℓ mechanisms.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: However, we prove that if δ 0 = 0, concurrent parallel composition behaves just like non-interactive parallel composition:
texts.text: However, we prove that if δ 0 = 0, concurrent parallel composition behaves just like non-interactive parallel composition:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Theorem 1.5 (concurrent parallel composition of pure DP continual mechanisms, informally stated) . For every ϵ 0 ≥ 0 and integers k ∈ N , the concurrent k -sparse parallel composition of ( ϵ 0 , 0) -DP mechanisms satisfies the same ( ϵ, δ ) -DP guarantees as the composition of k noninteractive ( ϵ 0 , 0) -DP mechanisms.
texts.text: Theorem 1.5 (concurrent parallel composition of pure DP continual mechanisms, informally stated) . For every ϵ 0 ≥ 0 and integers k ∈ N , the concurrent k -sparse parallel composition of ( ϵ 0 , 0) -DP mechanisms satisfies the same ( ϵ, δ ) -DP guarantees as the composition of k noninteractive ( ϵ 0 , 0) -DP mechanisms.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We note that the constraint of pure DP is only on the mechanisms being composed; we allow δ > 0 in the final privacy guarantee, so we can still, for example, recover the advanced composition bound applied to pure DP mechanisms. Like Theorem 1.3, we also prove a generalization of Theorem 1.5 for the case of composing mechanisms M i with differing ( ϵ i , δ i ) parameters.
texts.text: We note that the constraint of pure DP is only on the mechanisms being composed; we allow δ > 0 in the final privacy guarantee, so we can still, for example, recover the advanced composition bound applied to pure DP mechanisms. Like Theorem 1.3, we also prove a generalization of Theorem 1.5 for the case of composing mechanisms M i with differing ( ϵ i , δ i ) parameters.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To prove this theorem, we add a twist to the concurrent composition theorems for interactive mechanisms. Specifically, the papers [20, 18] proved their composition theorems for pure and approximate DP by showing that for every ( ϵ 0 , δ 0 )-DP interactive mechanism M and pair x 0 ∼ x 1 of adjacent datasets, M can be simulated as an interactive post-processing of the noninteractive ( ϵ 0 , δ 0 )-DP randomized response mechanism RR ϵ 0 ,δ 0 . That is, there is an IPM T (with no input) such that for each b ∈ { 0 , 1 } , M ( x b ) is equivalent to T ◦ ∗ RR ϵ 0 ,δ 0 ( b ).
texts.text: To prove this theorem, we add a twist to the concurrent composition theorems for interactive mechanisms. Specifically, the papers [20, 18] proved their composition theorems for pure and approximate DP by showing that for every ( ϵ 0 , δ 0 )-DP interactive mechanism M and pair x 0 ∼ x 1 of adjacent datasets, M can be simulated as an interactive post-processing of the noninteractive ( ϵ 0 , δ 0 )-DP randomized response mechanism RR ϵ 0 ,δ 0 . That is, there is an IPM T (with no input) such that for each b ∈ { 0 , 1 } , M ( x b ) is equivalent to T ◦ ∗ RR ϵ 0 ,δ 0 ( b ).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We show that when δ 0 = 0, the use of RR ϵ 0 ,δ 0 by T can be delayed until the time that it receives a query that would have different answer distributions under M ( x 0 ) and M ( x 1 ). While intuitive, Theorem 1.4 tells us that this fact fails when δ 0 > 0.
texts.text: We show that when δ 0 = 0, the use of RR ϵ 0 ,δ 0 by T can be delayed until the time that it receives a query that would have different answer distributions under M ( x 0 ) and M ( x 1 ). While intuitive, Theorem 1.4 tells us that this fact fails when δ 0 > 0.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: An Algorithmic Application. Composition theorems for differential privacy have two main uses. One is to bound the accumulated privacy loss when DP systems are used repeatedly over time. Another is to facilitate the design of complex DP algorithms from simpler ones. The concurrent composition theorems for interactive mechanisms have already found an application in the privacy analysis of matching algorithms in local edge differential privacy model [4]. (This work also has a result in the continual observation model, but the privacy is only proven for oblivious adversaries.)
texts.text: An Algorithmic Application. Composition theorems for differential privacy have two main uses. One is to bound the accumulated privacy loss when DP systems are used repeatedly over time. Another is to facilitate the design of complex DP algorithms from simpler ones. The concurrent composition theorems for interactive mechanisms have already found an application in the privacy analysis of matching algorithms in local edge differential privacy model [4]. (This work also has a result in the continual observation model, but the privacy is only proven for oblivious adversaries.)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Our concurrent composition theorem allows for a simpler and more modular privacy analysis of continual mechanisms built out of other continual mechanisms. In particular, we give a new privacy proof for the continual monotone histogram mechanism of Henzinger, Sricharan, and Steiner [12]. This mechanism involves concurrent executions of a simpler continual histogram mechanism and multiple instantations of the Sparse Vector Technique mechanism, and the original privacy analysis was rather intricate even for oblivious adversaries (because the mechanism itself is adaptive in its queries and dataset updates to the building-block mechanisms). With our concurrent composition theorems (both Theorem 1.2 and Theorem 1.5), we obtain a much simpler privacy proof. Moreover, for the case of approximate DP, the original proof was only for oblivious adversaries, whereas our proof also applies to adaptive adversaries.
texts.text: Our concurrent composition theorem allows for a simpler and more modular privacy analysis of continual mechanisms built out of other continual mechanisms. In particular, we give a new privacy proof for the continual monotone histogram mechanism of Henzinger, Sricharan, and Steiner [12]. This mechanism involves concurrent executions of a simpler continual histogram mechanism and multiple instantations of the Sparse Vector Technique mechanism, and the original privacy analysis was rather intricate even for oblivious adversaries (because the mechanism itself is adaptive in its queries and dataset updates to the building-block mechanisms). With our concurrent composition theorems (both Theorem 1.2 and Theorem 1.5), we obtain a much simpler privacy proof. Moreover, for the case of approximate DP, the original proof was only for oblivious adversaries, whereas our proof also applies to adaptive adversaries.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: General Conditions for Privacy Analysis of Continual Mechanisms. In the above application, the adversary does not directly interact with the sub-mechanisms, but rather it is the 'main' HSS mechanism M that issues messages to its sub-mechanisms, based on the messages it receives. (See Figure 4 for an illustration.) These messages are not necessarily identical to the messages that M receives. Instead based on the input that M receives from an analyst or an adversary (passed through V [ f ] ◦ ∗ I ( · )), it generates an input sequence for its sub-mechanisms M i that, on neighboring inputs to M , must be identical or neighboring for their respective verification functions f i . Note that M may fix the set of sub-mechanisms at initialization or select these mechanisms adaptively over time. For example, in the application above, the continual monotone histogram mechanism maintains d fi xed binary counters while adaptively instantiating an unbounded number of SVT and Laplace mechanisms as need.
texts.text: General Conditions for Privacy Analysis of Continual Mechanisms. In the above application, the adversary does not directly interact with the sub-mechanisms, but rather it is the 'main' HSS mechanism M that issues messages to its sub-mechanisms, based on the messages it receives. (See Figure 4 for an illustration.) These messages are not necessarily identical to the messages that M receives. Instead based on the input that M receives from an analyst or an adversary (passed through V [ f ] ◦ ∗ I ( · )), it generates an input sequence for its sub-mechanisms M i that, on neighboring inputs to M , must be identical or neighboring for their respective verification functions f i . Note that M may fix the set of sub-mechanisms at initialization or select these mechanisms adaptively over time. For example, in the application above, the continual monotone histogram mechanism maintains d fi xed binary counters while adaptively instantiating an unbounded number of SVT and Laplace mechanisms as need.

Moreover, in contrast to concurrent composition of interactive mechanisms, M has direct access to the dataset updates, which cannot be revealed without differentially private processing (which needs to be done by the sub-mechanisms). We give a set of conditions for M and prove a theorem that shows that under these conditions the privacy of M can directly be computed by applying our concurrent composition theorems to

texts.label: text
texts.orig: the continual sub-mechanisms. The general idea is that M should only use its input sequence to generate inputs for the differentially private sub-mechanisms and any other action of M , such as creating new sub-mechanism or generating a response, should be taken based only on the privatized outputs of the sub-mechanisms. This will ensure that M does not use any 'privacy budget' in addition to the privacy budget used by the sub-mechanisms. For example, if M executes k continual sub-mechanisms and, upon receiving neighboring inputs, issues neighboring message sequences for the sub-mechanisms, then the privacy of M equals the privacy of the concurrent composition of the sub-mechanism, as computed by Theorem 1.2. The same holds for the concurrent k -sparse parallel composition of sub-mechanisms, as computed in Theorems 1.3 and 1.5.

The same holds for the concurrent k -sparse parallel composition of sub-mechanisms, as computed in Theorems 1.3 and 1.5.

texts.orig: Our Variants of Concurrent Composition. Here we elaborate on the different types of concurrent composition that we study in this paper, with more general and more precise formulations than described above.
texts.text: Our Variants of Concurrent Composition. Here we elaborate on the different types of concurrent composition that we study in this paper, with more general and more precise formulations than described above.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: (1) Concurrent Composition of Continual Mechanisms: In the concurrent composition of fixed continual mechanism , k continual mechanisms M 1 , . . . , M k are fixed at the beginning, and an adversary adaptively issues pairs of messages for them such that for each M i , the sequences formed by the first messages of all message pairs and the sequence formed by the second messages of all message pairs are neighboring. A secret bit b ∈ { 0 , 1 } determines whether each mechanism receives the first or second sequence from each pair. The adversary's goal is to guess b using the answers of all k mechanisms. Theorem 4.10, which is the formal statement of Theorem 1.2 above, analyzes the privacy of this composition.
texts.text: Concurrent Composition of Continual Mechanisms: In the concurrent composition of fixed continual mechanism , k continual mechanisms M 1 , . . . , M k are fixed at the beginning, and an adversary adaptively issues pairs of messages for them such that for each M i , the sequences formed by the first messages of all message pairs and the sequence formed by the second messages of all message pairs are neighboring. A secret bit b ∈ { 0 , 1 } determines whether each mechanism receives the first or second sequence from each pair. The adversary's goal is to guess b using the answers of all k mechanisms. Theorem 4.10, which is the formal statement of Theorem 1.2 above, analyzes the privacy of this composition.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A natural extension is the concurrent composition of continual mechanism with fixed parameters , where instead of fixing the mechanisms themselves, k privacy parameter pairs ( ϵ 1 , δ 1 ) , . . . , ( ϵ k , δ k ) are fixed in advance. The adversary then adaptively selects mechanisms M 1 , . . . , M k over time, ensuring that, for each 1 ≤ i ≤ k , the mechanism M i is ( ϵ i , δ i )-DP. The privacy guarantee for this variant is the same as Theorem 4.10 and is stated in Theorem 4.12.
texts.text: A natural extension is the concurrent composition of continual mechanism with fixed parameters , where instead of fixing the mechanisms themselves, k privacy parameter pairs ( ϵ 1 , δ 1 ) , . . . , ( ϵ k , δ k ) are fixed in advance. The adversary then adaptively selects mechanisms M 1 , . . . , M k over time, ensuring that, for each 1 ≤ i ≤ k , the mechanism M i is ( ϵ i , δ i )-DP. The privacy guarantee for this variant is the same as Theorem 4.10 and is stated in Theorem 4.12.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: (2) Concurrent k -Sparse Parallel Composition of Continual Mechanisms: This composition generalizes the concurrent composition of continual mechanisms with k fi xed parameters by permitting the creation of an arbitrary number of continual mechanisms. As before, k privacy parameters ( ϵ 1 , δ 1 ) , . . . , ( ϵ k , δ k ) are fixed at the beginning. The adversary adaptively creates a potentially unlimited number of continual mechanisms and issues pairs of messages to each mechanism such that for all but at most k of these mechanisms, each message pair consists of identical messages. Let M 1 , . . . , M k denote the exceptional mechanisms, which may receive differing message sequences. For each 1 ≤ i ≤ k , the mechanism M i must be ( ϵ i , δ i )-DP, and the sequences of the first and second messages for M i must be neighboring. As in previous compositions, a secret bit b determines whether each mechanism receives the first or second message from each pair, and the adversary seeks to guess b based on the outputs. Theorem 1.5 analyzes this composition in the special case where δ 1 = · · · = δ k = 0 and ϵ 1 = · · · = ϵ k = ϵ 0 . The general version for non-identical ϵ i parameters is given in Theorem 5.2.
texts.text: Concurrent k -Sparse Parallel Composition of Continual Mechanisms: This composition generalizes the concurrent composition of continual mechanisms with k fi xed parameters by permitting the creation of an arbitrary number of continual mechanisms. As before, k privacy parameters ( ϵ 1 , δ 1 ) , . . . , ( ϵ k , δ k ) are fixed at the beginning. The adversary adaptively creates a potentially unlimited number of continual mechanisms and issues pairs of messages to each mechanism such that for all but at most k of these mechanisms, each message pair consists of identical messages. Let M 1 , . . . , M k denote the exceptional mechanisms, which may receive differing message sequences. For each 1 ≤ i ≤ k , the mechanism M i must be ( ϵ i , δ i )-DP, and the sequences of the first and second messages for M i must be neighboring. As in previous compositions, a secret bit b determines whether each mechanism receives the first or second message from each pair, and the adversary seeks to guess b based on the outputs. Theorem 1.5 analyzes this composition in the special case where δ 1 = · · · = δ k = 0 and ϵ 1 = · · · = ϵ k = ϵ 0 . The general version for non-identical ϵ i parameters is given in Theorem 5.2.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: (3) Concurrent k -Sparse Parallel Composition of Interactive Mechanisms: In this composition, the adversary creates an unbounded number of interactive mechanisms over time, instead of continual ones. As before, k privacy parameter pairs ( ϵ 1 , δ 1 ) , . . . , ( ϵ k , δ k ) are fixed in advance. Each time the adversary creates an interactive mechanism, they also select a pair of neighboring datasets for it. Instead of message pairs, the adversary adaptively sends (single) queries to each mechanism. A secret bit b determines whether the mechanism is run on the first or second dataset of its respective pair. The adversary then tries to infer b from the responses of all mechanisms. Theorem 1.3 analyzes the privacy of this composition
texts.text: Concurrent k -Sparse Parallel Composition of Interactive Mechanisms: In this composition, the adversary creates an unbounded number of interactive mechanisms over time, instead of continual ones. As before, k privacy parameter pairs ( ϵ 1 , δ 1 ) , . . . , ( ϵ k , δ k ) are fixed in advance. Each time the adversary creates an interactive mechanism, they also select a pair of neighboring datasets for it. Instead of message pairs, the adversary adaptively sends (single) queries to each mechanism. A secret bit b determines whether the mechanism is run on the first or second dataset of its respective pair. The adversary then tries to infer b from the responses of all mechanisms. Theorem 1.3 analyzes the privacy of this composition

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The paper is organized as follows. Section 2 introduces all necessary notation, including noninteractive mechanisms, interactive mechanisms, and interactive post-processing mechanism. Section 3 defines continual mechanisms, identifiers I , verification functions f , verifiers V and differential privacy for continual mechanisms. Concurrent composition of continual mechanisms is defined in Section 4 and our first concurrent composition theorem is proven. Section 5 introduces concurrent k -sparse parallel composition of continual mechanisms and interactive mechanisms, proves the theorems for this type of composition and shows the counterexample. In Section 6, we state the conditions and the theorem for analyzing the privacy of complex continual mechanisms that use non-interactive, interactive and continual sub-mechanisms. Finally, in Section 7, we apply our theorem to analyze the privacy of the continual histogram mechanism of [12]. Appendix A presents our extension of Lyu's result [18] to infinite answer spaces and an unbounded number of rounds of interaction.
texts.text: The paper is organized as follows. Section 2 introduces all necessary notation, including noninteractive mechanisms, interactive mechanisms, and interactive post-processing mechanism. Section 3 defines continual mechanisms, identifiers I , verification functions f , verifiers V and differential privacy for continual mechanisms. Concurrent composition of continual mechanisms is defined in Section 4 and our first concurrent composition theorem is proven. Section 5 introduces concurrent k -sparse parallel composition of continual mechanisms and interactive mechanisms, proves the theorems for this type of composition and shows the counterexample. In Section 6, we state the conditions and the theorem for analyzing the privacy of complex continual mechanisms that use non-interactive, interactive and continual sub-mechanisms. Finally, in Section 7, we apply our theorem to analyze the privacy of the continual histogram mechanism of [12]. Appendix A presents our extension of Lyu's result [18] to infinite answer spaces and an unbounded number of rounds of interaction.

To define differential privacy, we require the definition of indistinguishability for random variables:

For example, we often consider two tabular datasets x, x ′ to be neighboring if they differ only by the presence or absence of a single record. Differential privacy for NIMs is defined as follows:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Definition 2.2 (( ϵ, δ )-DP for NIMs [6]) . For ϵ ≥ 0 and 0 ≤ δ ≤ 1 , a randomized mechanism M : X → Y is ( ϵ, δ )-differentially private (or ( ϵ, δ )-DP for short) with respect to (w.r.t.) a neighbor relation ∼ if for every two datasets x 0 , x 1 ∈ X satisfying x 0 ∼ x 1 , the random variables M ( x 0 ) and M ( x 1 ) are ( ϵ, δ ) -indistinguishable.
texts.text: Definition 2.2 (( ϵ, δ )-DP for NIMs [6]) . For ϵ ≥ 0 and 0 ≤ δ ≤ 1 , a randomized mechanism M : X → Y is ( ϵ, δ )-differentially private (or ( ϵ, δ )-DP for short) with respect to (w.r.t.) a neighbor relation ∼ if for every two datasets x 0 , x 1 ∈ X satisfying x 0 ∼ x 1 , the random variables M ( x 0 ) and M ( x 1 ) are ( ϵ, δ ) -indistinguishable.

texts.orig: The state of an IM M serves as its memory. The initial state of M can include secret information, such as a dataset x ∈ X , or it can simply be an empty memory. Thus, in some cases, the size of the initial state space S init M is as large as the family of datasets X , while in some other cases, S init M is a singleton consisting of a single state representing the empty memory.

texts.orig: 2.2.1. Interaction of IMs. Two interactive mechanisms M 1 and M 2 satisfying Q M 1 = A M 2 and Q M 2 = A M 1 can interact with each other through message passing. For initial states s 1 ∈ S init M 1 and s 2 ∈ S init M 2 , the interaction between M 1 ( s 1 ) and M 2 ( s 2 ) is defined as follows: It starts with round 1. Mechanism M 1 ( s 1 ) sends a message to M 2 ( s 2 ) in the odd rounds, and M 2 ( s 2 ) responds in the subsequent even rounds. The interaction continues until one of the mechanisms halts the communication. A mechanism halts the communication by sending the specific message halt at its round.
texts.text: 2.2.1. Interaction of IMs. Two interactive mechanisms M 1 and M 2 satisfying Q M 1 = A M 2 and Q M 2 = A M 1 can interact with each other through message passing. For initial states s 1 ∈ S init M 1 and s 2 ∈ S init M 2 , the interaction between M 1 ( s 1 ) and M 2 ( s 2 ) is defined as follows: It starts with round 1. Mechanism M 1 ( s 1 ) sends a message to M 2 ( s 2 ) in the odd rounds, and M 2 ( s 2 ) responds in the subsequent even rounds. The interaction continues until one of the mechanisms halts the communication. A mechanism halts the communication by sending the specific message halt at its round.

texts.orig: Definition 2.10 (Equivalent IMs) . Let M and M ′ be two interactive mechanisms with Q M = Q M ′ and A M = A M ′ . Let s and s ′ be initial states for M and M ′ , respectively. texts.text: Existing works [20, 21, 18, 10] view the initial state of M is a dataset, i.e., S init M = X , and (as is usual) they define neighbor relations as binary relations on X . However, for us it will be convenient to conceptually separate S init M from the data (which is changing continuously over time), but still define DP w.r.t. neighbor relations on S init M . This leads to the following definition of differential privacy for IMs.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Definition 2.12 (( ϵ, δ )-Differential Privacy for IMs) . Let M be an interactive mechanism, and let ∼ be a neighbor relation on S init M . For ϵ ≥ 0 and 0 ≤ δ ≤ 1 , M is ( ϵ, δ ) -differentially private (or ( ϵ, δ ) -DP for short) w.r.t. ∼ against adaptive adversaries if for every pair of neighboring initial states s 0 , s 1 ∈ S init M satisfying s 0 ∼ s 1 and every adaptive adversary A , the random variables View( A , M ( s 0 )) and View( A , M ( s 1 )) are ( ϵ, δ ) -indistinguishable.
texts.text: Definition 2.12 (( ϵ, δ )-Differential Privacy for IMs) . Let M be an interactive mechanism, and let ∼ be a neighbor relation on S init M . For ϵ ≥ 0 and 0 ≤ δ ≤ 1 , M is ( ϵ, δ ) -differentially private (or ( ϵ, δ ) -DP for short) w.r.t. ∼ against adaptive adversaries if for every pair of neighboring initial states s 0 , s 1 ∈ S init M satisfying s 0 ∼ s 1 and every adaptive adversary A , the random variables View( A , M ( s 0 )) and View( A , M ( s 1 )) are ( ϵ, δ ) -indistinguishable.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: 2.3. Interactive Post-Processing (IPM). For NIMs, a randomized post-processing function T : Y → Z maps the output of a mechanism N : X → Y to an element of Z . Differential privacy for NIMs is known to be preserved under post-processing.
texts.text: 2.3. Interactive Post-Processing (IPM). For NIMs, a randomized post-processing function T : Y → Z maps the output of a mechanism N : X → Y to an element of Z . Differential privacy for NIMs is known to be preserved under post-processing.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Haney et al. [10] generalize post-processing functions to interactive post-processing mechanisms (IPMs) as follows and show that interactive post-processing preserves privacy guarantees for IMs.
texts.text: Haney et al. [10] generalize post-processing functions to interactive post-processing mechanisms (IPMs) as follows and show that interactive post-processing preserves privacy guarantees for IMs.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Definition 2.13 (Interactive Post-Processing Mechanism (IPM) [10]) . An interactive post-processing mechanism (IPM) is a stateful randomized algorithm P : S P × ( ( { L } × Q L P ) ∪ ( { R } × Q R P ) ) → S P × ( ( { L } × A L P ) ∪ ( { R } × A R P ) ) that stands between two interactive mechanisms, referred to as the left and right mechanisms, communicating with them via message passing. The state space of P is denoted by S P , while Q L P and Q R P represent the sets of possible messages received by P from the left and right mechanisms, respectively. Similarly, A L P and A R P denote the sets of possible messages P sends to the left and right mechanisms. P randomly maps its current state s ∈ S P , an indicator v ∈ { L , R } specifying whether the incoming message is from the left or right mechanism, and a message m ∈ Q L P ∪ Q R P to a new state s ′ ∈ S P , an indicator v ′ ∈ { L , R } specifying whether the outgoing message is for the left or right mechanism, and a message m ′ ∈ A L P ∪ A R P . This is described by ( s ′ , v ′ , m ′ ) ←P ( s, v, m ) .
texts.text: Definition 2.13 (Interactive Post-Processing Mechanism (IPM) [10]) . An interactive post-processing mechanism (IPM) is a stateful randomized algorithm P : S P × ( ( { L } × Q L P ) ∪ ( { R } × Q R P ) ) → S P × ( ( { L } × A L P ) ∪ ( { R } × A R P ) ) that stands between two interactive mechanisms, referred to as the left and right mechanisms, communicating with them via message passing. The state space of P is denoted by S P , while Q L P and Q R P represent the sets of possible messages received by P from the left and right mechanisms, respectively. Similarly, A L P and A R P denote the sets of possible messages P sends to the left and right mechanisms. P randomly maps its current state s ∈ S P , an indicator v ∈ { L , R } specifying whether the incoming message is from the left or right mechanism, and a message m ∈ Q L P ∪ Q R P to a new state s ′ ∈ S P , an indicator v ′ ∈ { L , R } specifying whether the outgoing message is for the left or right mechanism, and a message m ′ ∈ A L P ∪ A R P . This is described by ( s ′ , v ′ , m ′ ) ←P ( s, v, m ) .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The post-processing of an NIM N by a non-interactive post-processing function T , denoted as T ◦N , is an NIM that first runs N on the input dataset and then applies T to N 's output, returning T 's output. In contrast, the post-processing of an IM M by an IPM P , denoted by P◦ ∗ M , is an IM that runs P and M internally. In an interaction with another IM A , P◦ ∗ M forwards its input messages from A to P as left messages. Then, P and M communicate multiple times, with M acting as the right mechanism, until P returns a response for its left mechanism, at which point P◦ ∗ M returns this response to A . Note that P may return an answer for its left mechanism

texts.orig: Definition 2.14 (Post-Processing of an IM M by an IPM P ( P◦ ∗ M )[10]) . Let M be an IM and P an IPM such that Q R P = A M and A R P = Q M . The post-processing of M by P is an interactive mechanism P◦ ∗ M : S P◦ ∗ M × Q L P → S P◦ ∗ M × A L P , defined in Algorithm 1. The initial state space of P◦ ∗ M equals S init P◦ ∗ M = S init M × S init P . For readability, when P◦ ∗ M is initialized with the state ( s M , s P ) ∈ S init P◦ ∗ M , we denote it as P ( s P ) ◦ ∗ M ( s M ) instead of ( P◦ ∗ M ) ( ( s M , s P ) ) .
texts.text: Definition 2.14 (Post-Processing of an IM M by an IPM P ( P◦ ∗ M )[10]) . Let M be an IM and P an IPM such that Q R P = A M and A R P = Q M . The post-processing of M by P is an interactive mechanism P◦ ∗ M : S P◦ ∗ M × Q L P → S P◦ ∗ M × A L P , defined in Algorithm 1. The initial state space of P◦ ∗ M equals S init P◦ ∗ M = S init M × S init P . For readability, when P◦ ∗ M is initialized with the state ( s M , s P ) ∈ S init P◦ ∗ M , we denote it as P ( s P ) ◦ ∗ M ( s M ) instead of ( P◦ ∗ M ) ( ( s M , s P ) ) .

texts.orig: procedure ( P◦ ∗ M )( s, m ): ( s M , s P ) ← s ( s P , v, m ′ ) ←P ( s P , L , m ) while v = R do ( s M , m ) ←M ( s M , m ′ ) ( s P , v, m ′ ) ←P ( s P , R , m ) end while return (( s M , s P ) , m ′ ) end procedure

texts.orig: Haney et al. [10] shows that if an IM M is ( ϵ, δ )-differentially private, then for any IPM P with a singleton initial state space, P◦ ∗ M is also ( ϵ, δ )-differentially private. This result is formally stated below.
texts.text: Haney et al. [10] shows that if an IM M is ( ϵ, δ )-differentially private, then for any IPM P with a singleton initial state space, P◦ ∗ M is also ( ϵ, δ )-differentially private. This result is formally stated below.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Lemma 2.15 (Post-Processing Lemma for IMs [10]) . Let M be an IM M with an initial state space S init M and P be an IPM with a singleton initial state space S init P = { s } . Let ∼ be a neighbor relation on S init M . Define a binary relation ∼ ∗ on S init P◦ ∗ M such that two initial states ( s 1 M , s ) and ( s 2 M , s ) satisfy ( s 1 M , s ) ∼ ∗ ( s 2 M , s ) if and only if s 1 M ∼ s 2 M . If M is ( ϵ, δ ) -DP w.r.t. the neighbor relation ∼ against adaptive adversaries, then P◦ ∗ M is also ( ϵ, δ ) -DP w.r.t. the neighbor relation ∼ ∗ against adaptive adversaries. 2
texts.text: Lemma 2.15 (Post-Processing Lemma for IMs [10]) . Let M be an IM M with an initial state space S init M and P be an IPM with a singleton initial state space S init P = { s } . Let ∼ be a neighbor relation on S init M . Define a binary relation ∼ ∗ on S init P◦ ∗ M such that two initial states ( s 1 M , s ) and ( s 2 M , s ) satisfy ( s 1 M , s ) ∼ ∗ ( s 2 M , s ) if and only if s 1 M ∼ s 2 M . If M is ( ϵ, δ ) -DP w.r.t. the neighbor relation ∼ against adaptive adversaries, then P◦ ∗ M is also ( ϵ, δ ) -DP w.r.t. the neighbor relation ∼ ∗ against adaptive adversaries. 2

texts.orig: Definition 2.16 (Chain of IPMs) . Let P 1 and P 2 be two IPMs satisfying Q R P 1 = A L P 2 and A R P 1 = Q L P 2 . P 1 ◦ ∗ P 2 is an IPM with state space S P 1 ◦ ∗ P 2 = S P 1 × S P 2 , left query space Q L P 1 ◦ ∗ P 2 = Q L P 1 , left answer space A L P 1 ◦ ∗ P 2 = A L P 1 , right query space Q R P 1 ◦ ∗ P 2 = Q R P 2 , and right answer space A R P 1 ◦ ∗ P 2 = A R P 2 . This mechanism is formally defined in Algorithm 2. For readability, when P 1 ◦ ∗ P 2 is initialized with ( s 1 , s 1 ) ∈ S init P 1 ◦ ∗ P 2 , we denote it as P 1 ( s 1 ) ◦ ∗ P 2 ( s 2 ) instead of ( P 1 ◦ ∗ P 2 ) ( ( s 1 , s 2 ) ) .
texts.text: Definition 2.16 (Chain of IPMs) . Let P 1 and P 2 be two IPMs satisfying Q R P 1 = A L P 2 and A R P 1 = Q L P 2 . P 1 ◦ ∗ P 2 is an IPM with state space S P 1 ◦ ∗ P 2 = S P 1 × S P 2 , left query space Q L P 1 ◦ ∗ P 2 = Q L P 1 , left answer space A L P 1 ◦ ∗ P 2 = A L P 1 , right query space Q R P 1 ◦ ∗ P 2 = Q R P 2 , and right answer space A R P 1 ◦ ∗ P 2 = A R P 2 . This mechanism is formally defined in Algorithm 2. For readability, when P 1 ◦ ∗ P 2 is initialized with ( s 1 , s 1 ) ∈ S init P 1 ◦ ∗ P 2 , we denote it as P 1 ( s 1 ) ◦ ∗ P 2 ( s 2 ) instead of ( P 1 ◦ ∗ P 2 ) ( ( s 1 , s 2 ) ) .

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3. Continual Mechanisms
texts.text: 3. Continual Mechanisms

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this section, we introduce continual mechanisms (CMs) and propose a general definition of differential privacy (DP) for them. This definition unifies and extends DP for IMs and DP under continual observation. Its generality and simplicity allow us to prove (filter) concurrent composition theorems for CMs without requiring complicated proofs. We later apply these theorems to simplify existing analyses and strengthen results in the continual observation setting.
texts.text: In this section, we introduce continual mechanisms (CMs) and propose a general definition of differential privacy (DP) for them. This definition unifies and extends DP for IMs and DP under continual observation. Its generality and simplicity allow us to prove (filter) concurrent composition theorems for CMs without requiring complicated proofs. We later apply these theorems to simplify existing analyses and strengthen results in the continual observation setting.

texts.label: footnote
texts.orig: 2 In [10], the definitions of IMs and IPMs are more restrictive. Specifically, for any IM M , S M = Q M = A M = { 0 , 1 } ∗ , and for any IPM P , S P = M P = { 0 , 1 } ∗ ; However, their results do not rely on these restrictions, and the interactive post-processing lemma holds for arbitrary sets S M , Q M , A M , S P , and M P .

Specifically, for any IM M , S M = Q M = A M = { 0 , 1 } ∗ , and for any IPM P , S P = M P = { 0 , 1 } ∗ ; However, their results do not rely on these restrictions, and the interactive post-processing lemma holds for arbitrary sets S M , Q M , A M , S P , and M P .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This section is based on two crucial observations: (i) A message, as sent or received by IMs and IPMs, is a general and powerful concept capable of encoding various types of instructions, and (ii) the essence of DP is to protect the privacy of a single bit that distinguishes neighboring states, sequences, etc. The second observation has been used many times in the DP literature and was specifically used in [13] to model adaptivity in continual observation. Using these key observations, we first reformulate the definition of DP for IMs and continual observation. Then, inspired by the new formulations, we introduce continual mechanisms (CMs) and formally define DP in this setting.
texts.text: This section is based on two crucial observations: (i) A message, as sent or received by IMs and IPMs, is a general and powerful concept capable of encoding various types of instructions, and (ii) the essence of DP is to protect the privacy of a single bit that distinguishes neighboring states, sequences, etc. The second observation has been used many times in the DP literature and was specifically used in [13] to model adaptivity in continual observation. Using these key observations, we first reformulate the definition of DP for IMs and continual observation. Then, inspired by the new formulations, we introduce continual mechanisms (CMs) and formally define DP in this setting.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: 3.1. An Alternative Definition of DP for IMs. By Definition 2.12, an interactive mechanism M with initial state space S init M is ( ϵ, δ )-DP w.r.t. a neighboring relation ∼ , if for any two initial states s 0 , s 1 ∈ S init M such that s 0 ∼ s 1 and any adversary A , the view of A in the interaction with M ( s 0 ) and M ( s 1 ) is ( ϵ, δ )-indistinguishable. This means that for the worst-case (i.e., adversarial) choice of neighboring initial states, an adversary by adaptively asking queries should not be able to determine whether M is running on s 0 or s 1 .
texts.text: 3.1. An Alternative Definition of DP for IMs. By Definition 2.12, an interactive mechanism M with initial state space S init M is ( ϵ, δ )-DP w.r.t. a neighboring relation ∼ , if for any two initial states s 0 , s 1 ∈ S init M such that s 0 ∼ s 1 and any adversary A , the view of A in the interaction with M ( s 0 ) and M ( s 1 ) is ( ϵ, δ )-indistinguishable. This means that for the worst-case (i.e., adversarial) choice of neighboring initial states, an adversary by adaptively asking queries should not be able to determine whether M is running on s 0 or s 1 .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In an alternative definition, an adversary sends a pair of neighboring initial states ( s 0 , s 1 ) as its first message and adaptively asks a sequence of queries q 1 , q 2 , . . . in the subsequent messages. In this setting, M receives one of s 0 and s 1 as its first message and responds with an acknowledgment message ⊤ . Thereafter, M answers queries based on the received state, and the goal of the adversary is to determine whether M was initialized with s 0 or s 1 . In this definition, the actual initial state of M (not the one received as the first message) carries no secret information and serves merely as an empty memory. Therefore, the initial state space of M consists of a single fixed initial state.
texts.text: In an alternative definition, an adversary sends a pair of neighboring initial states ( s 0 , s 1 ) as its first message and adaptively asks a sequence of queries q 1 , q 2 , . . . in the subsequent messages. In this setting, M receives one of s 0 and s 1 as its first message and responds with an acknowledgment message ⊤ . Thereafter, M answers queries based on the received state, and the goal of the adversary is to determine whether M was initialized with s 0 or s 1 . In this definition, the actual initial state of M (not the one received as the first message) carries no secret information and serves merely as an empty memory. Therefore, the initial state space of M consists of a single fixed initial state.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To formalize this, we restrict attention to adversaries that send a pair of neighboring initial states ( s 0 , s 1 ) as their first message and pairs of identical queries ( q i , q i ) in its subsequent messages. We also define an IPM I with the initial state space S init I = { 0 , 1 } to stand between the adversary and M , filtering the pairs based on its, i.e., I 's, initial state. Specifically, for b ∈ { 0 , 1 } , in an interaction between an adversary A and I ( b ) ◦ ∗ M , upon receiving a message ( m 0 , m 1 ) from A , I ( b ) forwards m b to M and returns M 's answer unchanged to A . With this formulation, an interactive mechanism M is ( ϵ, δ )-DP if for any adversary of the above form, the random variables View( A , I [0] ◦ ∗ M ) and View( A , I [1] ◦ ∗ M ) are ( ϵ, δ )-indistinguishable.
texts.text: To formalize this, we restrict attention to adversaries that send a pair of neighboring initial states ( s 0 , s 1 ) as their first message and pairs of identical queries ( q i , q i ) in its subsequent messages. We also define an IPM I with the initial state space S init I = { 0 , 1 } to stand between the adversary and M , filtering the pairs based on its, i.e., I 's, initial state. Specifically, for b ∈ { 0 , 1 } , in an interaction between an adversary A and I ( b ) ◦ ∗ M , upon receiving a message ( m 0 , m 1 ) from A , I ( b ) forwards m b to M and returns M 's answer unchanged to A . With this formulation, an interactive mechanism M is ( ϵ, δ )-DP if for any adversary of the above form, the random variables View( A , I [0] ◦ ∗ M ) and View( A , I [1] ◦ ∗ M ) are ( ϵ, δ )-indistinguishable.
