texts.text: Actually, we will enforce the restrictions on the adversary also by an IPM, which we call a verifier V . The verifier checks whether the initial message is a pair of neighboring datasets and the subsequent messages of the adversary are of the form ( q, q ); only if these checks pass, are the messages forwarded to I .

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 3.2. An Alternative Definition of DP under Continual Observation. Dwork, Naor, Pitassi, and Rothblum [7] introduced differential privacy under continual observation , where the dataset is not given at the initialization time and instead evolves over time. In this model, the mechanism receives either an empty record or a data record at each step. If the record is non-empty, the mechanism adds it to its dataset. Then, whether the input record is empty or not, the mechanism answers a predetermined question about its current dataset.
texts.text: 3.2. An Alternative Definition of DP under Continual Observation. Dwork, Naor, Pitassi, and Rothblum [7] introduced differential privacy under continual observation , where the dataset is not given at the initialization time and instead evolves over time. In this model, the mechanism receives either an empty record or a data record at each step. If the record is non-empty, the mechanism adds it to its dataset. Then, whether the input record is empty or not, the mechanism answers a predetermined question about its current dataset.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To capture 'event-level privacy' (where the privacy unit is a single record), Dwork et al. [7] define neighboring as a binary relation on sequences of data and empty records: two sequences are neighboring if they are identical except for the presence or absence of a record in a single step. They called a mechanism ( ϵ, δ ) -DP under continual observation if the distributions of answer sequences are ( ϵ, δ )-indistinguishable for any two neighboring input sequences. This definition does not allow for data sequences that are adaptively chosen based on the answers released.
texts.text: To capture 'event-level privacy' (where the privacy unit is a single record), Dwork et al. [7] define neighboring as a binary relation on sequences of data and empty records: two sequences are neighboring if they are identical except for the presence or absence of a record in a single step. They called a mechanism ( ϵ, δ ) -DP under continual observation if the distributions of answer sequences are ( ϵ, δ )-indistinguishable for any two neighboring input sequences. This definition does not allow for data sequences that are adaptively chosen based on the answers released.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Jain, Raskhodnikova, Sivakumar, and Smith [13] formalized the definition of DP against adaptive adversaries in the continual observation model as follows: An adaptive adversary A chooses a single (data or empty) record at every step, except for one challenge step, selected by the adversary, where A sends a pair of records. They called a mechanism M ( ϵ, δ ) -differentially private against adaptive adversaries in the continual observation model if for any such adaptive adversary A , the distributions of M 's answer sequences are ( ϵ, δ )-indistinguishable when the first or the second element of the pair in the challenge step is handed to M .
texts.text: Jain, Raskhodnikova, Sivakumar, and Smith [13] formalized the definition of DP against adaptive adversaries in the continual observation model as follows: An adaptive adversary A chooses a single (data or empty) record at every step, except for one challenge step, selected by the adversary, where A sends a pair of records. They called a mechanism M ( ϵ, δ ) -differentially private against adaptive adversaries in the continual observation model if for any such adaptive adversary A , the distributions of M 's answer sequences are ( ϵ, δ )-indistinguishable when the first or the second element of the pair in the challenge step is handed to M .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A mechanism M in the continual observation model can be viewed as an IM with a single possible initial state, representing an empty dataset. The input message set of M consists of the empty record and all possible data records. Instead of having a challenge step, we can alternatively restrict to adversaries that send pairs of identical records at every step except for one. Under this formulation, the definition of differential privacy proposed by [13] can be restated as follows: A mechanism M is ( ϵ, δ )-DP differential privacy against adaptive adversaries if for every such adversary A , the random variables View( A , I [0] ◦ ∗ M ) and View( A , I [1] ◦ ∗ M ) are ( ϵ, δ )-indistinguishable, where I is the interactive post-processing mechanism discussed previously. Again, below we will enforce the restriction on the adversary via another IPM V .
texts.text: A mechanism M in the continual observation model can be viewed as an IM with a single possible initial state, representing an empty dataset. The input message set of M consists of the empty record and all possible data records. Instead of having a challenge step, we can alternatively restrict to adversaries that send pairs of identical records at every step except for one. Under this formulation, the definition of differential privacy proposed by [13] can be restated as follows: A mechanism M is ( ϵ, δ )-DP differential privacy against adaptive adversaries if for every such adversary A , the random variables View( A , I [0] ◦ ∗ M ) and View( A , I [1] ◦ ∗ M ) are ( ϵ, δ )-indistinguishable, where I is the interactive post-processing mechanism discussed previously. Again, below we will enforce the restriction on the adversary via another IPM V .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this alternative definition, all adversary messages are pairs of identical data or empty records, except for a single message that is a pair of different records. Recall that in the alternative definition of DP for IMs, the first message sent by an adversary must be a pair of neighboring initial datasets, while all subsequent adversary messages must be pairs of identical queries. Next, we generalize the definition of differential privacy by allowing arbitrary conditions on the sequence of pairs sent by adversaries, without restricting the content of the messages.
texts.text: In this alternative definition, all adversary messages are pairs of identical data or empty records, except for a single message that is a pair of different records. Recall that in the alternative definition of DP for IMs, the first message sent by an adversary must be a pair of neighboring initial datasets, while all subsequent adversary messages must be pairs of identical queries. Next, we generalize the definition of differential privacy by allowing arbitrary conditions on the sequence of pairs sent by adversaries, without restricting the content of the messages.

texts.text: 3.3. Introducing CMs. As discussed in the alternative formulation of IMs, we can assume that the initial state space of IMs is a singleton and encode its initial state as a message. A continual mechanism is defined as follows:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: Definition 3.1 (Continual Mechanism (CM)) . A continual mechanism (CM) is an interactive mechanism with a singleton initial state space.
texts.text: Definition 3.1 (Continual Mechanism (CM)) . A continual mechanism (CM) is an interactive mechanism with a singleton initial state space.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To the best of our knowledge, all mechanisms in DP literature can be represented as CMs:
texts.text: To the best of our knowledge, all mechanisms in DP literature can be represented as CMs:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Any non-interactive mechanism N , such as the Laplace mechanism, can be modeled as a CM that receives a dataset x as its first message and returns N ( x ) as a response. Upon receiving any subsequent input message, the CM representing N halts the communication.
texts.text: Any non-interactive mechanism N , such as the Laplace mechanism, can be modeled as a CM that receives a dataset x as its first message and returns N ( x ) as a response. Upon receiving any subsequent input message, the CM representing N halts the communication.

texts.orig: 3.4. DP for CMs. To formulate the general definition of DP for CMs, we introduce two IPMs: an identifier I and a verifier V . As described earlier, an identifier receives a secret bit b as its initial state and maps messages of the form ( m 0 , m 1 ) to m b . To avoid defining a new family of adversaries for each problem or model, we introduce verifiers .
texts.text: 3.4. DP for CMs. To formulate the general definition of DP for CMs, we introduce two IPMs: an identifier I and a verifier V . As described earlier, an identifier receives a secret bit b as its initial state and maps messages of the form ( m 0 , m 1 ) to m b . To avoid defining a new family of adversaries for each problem or model, we introduce verifiers .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Let M be a CM and I be an identifier for M . A verifier V with a verification function f , denoted by V [ f ], stands between an adversary A and the interactive mechanism I◦ ∗ M . Upon receiving a message from A , V [ f ] applies f to verify whether the sequence of pairs sent by the adversary up to that point satisfies a set of properties. Comparing the sequences formed by the first and second elements of the pairs, f can be used to encode a neighboring property. If the pair sequence satisfies the properties, V [ f ] forwards A 's message to I◦ ∗ M ; otherwise, it halts the communication. The formal definitions of identifiers, verification functions, and verifiers are given below.
texts.text: Let M be a CM and I be an identifier for M . A verifier V with a verification function f , denoted by V [ f ], stands between an adversary A and the interactive mechanism I◦ ∗ M . Upon receiving a message from A , V [ f ] applies f to verify whether the sequence of pairs sent by the adversary up to that point satisfies a set of properties. Comparing the sequences formed by the first and second elements of the pairs, f can be used to encode a neighboring property. If the pair sequence satisfies the properties, V [ f ] forwards A 's message to I◦ ∗ M ; otherwise, it halts the communication. The formal definitions of identifiers, verification functions, and verifiers are given below.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Definition 3.2 (Identifier I ) . An identifier I is an IPM with state space S I and initial state space S init I = { 0 , 1 } . I stands between two IMs, referred to as the left and right mechanisms. While there is no restriction on the format of messages received from the right mechanism, I only accepts messages of the form ( m 0 , m 1 ) from its left mechanism. Upon receiving a message ( m 0 , m 1 ) from the left mechanism, an instance of I with an initial state b ∈ { 0 , 1 } forwards m b to the right mechanism. Upon receiving a message from the right mechanism, I ( b ) forwards the message unchanged to the left mechanism.
texts.text: Definition 3.2 (Identifier I ) . An identifier I is an IPM with state space S I and initial state space S init I = { 0 , 1 } . I stands between two IMs, referred to as the left and right mechanisms. While there is no restriction on the format of messages received from the right mechanism, I only accepts messages of the form ( m 0 , m 1 ) from its left mechanism. Upon receiving a message ( m 0 , m 1 ) from the left mechanism, an instance of I with an initial state b ∈ { 0 , 1 } forwards m b to the right mechanism. Upon receiving a message from the right mechanism, I ( b ) forwards the message unchanged to the left mechanism.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Definition 3.3 (Verification Function) . A verification function f takes a sequence of messages as input and returns either ⊤ or ⊥ . A message sequence m 1 , . . . , m k is f -valid if f ( m 1 , . . . , m k ) = ⊤ .
texts.text: Definition 3.3 (Verification Function) . A verification function f takes a sequence of messages as input and returns either ⊤ or ⊥ . A message sequence m 1 , . . . , m k is f -valid if f ( m 1 , . . . , m k ) = ⊤ .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Remark 3.4. Since the set of all possible messages does not exist, we assume that all message spaces considered in this paper are subsets of some universal set U . For instance, in [10] , the universal set is taken to be { 0 , 1 } ∗ . The domain of verification functions is U ∗ .
texts.text: Remark 3.4. Since the set of all possible messages does not exist, we assume that all message spaces considered in this paper are subsets of some universal set U . For instance, in [10] , the universal set is taken to be { 0 , 1 } ∗ . The domain of verification functions is U ∗ .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Definition 3.5 (Verifier V ) . For a verification function f , a verifier V [ f ] is an IPM with the initial state space S init V [ f ] = { () } , where () represents an empty sequence. Since the initial state space of V [ f ] is a singleton, we denote an instance of V [ f ] with the initial state () as V [ f ] . V [ f ] stands between two IMs, referred to as the left and right mechanisms. The state of V [ f ] consists of the sequence of messages it has received from the left mechanism so far. Upon receiving a message m from the left mechanism, V [ f ] appends m to its state and applies the verification function f . If the sequence is f -valid, then V [ f ] forwards the message m to the right mechanism; otherwise, it halts the communication. V [ f ] forwards any message from the right mechanism unchanged to the left mechanism.
texts.text: Definition 3.5 (Verifier V ) . For a verification function f , a verifier V [ f ] is an IPM with the initial state space S init V [ f ] = { () } , where () represents an empty sequence. Since the initial state space of V [ f ] is a singleton, we denote an instance of V [ f ] with the initial state () as V [ f ] . V [ f ] stands between two IMs, referred to as the left and right mechanisms. The state of V [ f ] consists of the sequence of messages it has received from the left mechanism so far. Upon receiving a message m from the left mechanism, V [ f ] appends m to its state and applies the verification function f . If the sequence is f -valid, then V [ f ] forwards the message m to the right mechanism; otherwise, it halts the communication. V [ f ] forwards any message from the right mechanism unchanged to the left mechanism.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Remark 3.6. By definition, for any continual mechanism M and any verification function f , V [ f ] ◦ ∗ I◦ ∗ M is an IM with two possible initial states. The mechanism I◦ ∗ M accepts messages of the form ( m 0 , m 1 ) , where both m 0 and m 1 are in the query space Q M of M . Therefore, when analyzing V [ f ] ◦ ∗ I◦ ∗ M , we assume that the verification function f always returns ⊥ whenever a message in its input sequence is not in Q M × Q M , even if not explicitly say so.
texts.text: Remark 3.6. By definition, for any continual mechanism M and any verification function f , V [ f ] ◦ ∗ I◦ ∗ M is an IM with two possible initial states. The mechanism I◦ ∗ M accepts messages of the form ( m 0 , m 1 ) , where both m 0 and m 1 are in the query space Q M of M . Therefore, when analyzing V [ f ] ◦ ∗ I◦ ∗ M , we assume that the verification function f always returns ⊥ whenever a message in its input sequence is not in Q M × Q M , even if not explicitly say so.

texts.orig: Definition 3.7 (( ϵ, δ )-Differential Privacy for CMs) . Let M be a CM and f a verification function. We say that M is ( ϵ, δ )-differentially private (or ( ϵ, δ )-DP for short) w.r.t. f against adaptive adversaries if for any adversary A , the random variables View( A , V [ f ] ◦ ∗ I (0) ◦ ∗ M ) and View( A , V [ f ] ◦ ∗ I (1) ◦ ∗ M ) are ( ϵ, δ ) -indistinguishable.
texts.text: Definition 3.7 (( ϵ, δ )-Differential Privacy for CMs) . Let M be a CM and f a verification function. We say that M is ( ϵ, δ )-differentially private (or ( ϵ, δ )-DP for short) w.r.t. f against adaptive adversaries if for any adversary A , the random variables View( A , V [ f ] ◦ ∗ I (0) ◦ ∗ M ) and View( A , V [ f ] ◦ ∗ I (1) ◦ ∗ M ) are ( ϵ, δ ) -indistinguishable.

texts.orig: Corollary 3.8. A continual mechanism M is ( ϵ, δ ) -DP w.r.t. a verification function f against adaptive adversaries if and only if the interactive mechanism V [ f ] ◦ ∗ I◦ ∗ M is ( ϵ, δ ) -DP w.r.t. the neighboring relation ∼ 01 against adaptive adversaries.
texts.text: Corollary 3.8. A continual mechanism M is ( ϵ, δ ) -DP w.r.t. a verification function f against adaptive adversaries if and only if the interactive mechanism V [ f ] ◦ ∗ I◦ ∗ M is ( ϵ, δ ) -DP w.r.t. the neighboring relation ∼ 01 against adaptive adversaries.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: CMs vs. IMs. In principle, CMs are a special class of IMs with a singleton initial state space. Conversely, as described earlier, IMs can be represented by CMs. Both CMs and IMs are defined by a randomized function mapping a state and query message to another state and an answer message, where message is a general concept. The key distinction between CMs and IMs lies in how differential privacy is defined for each.
texts.text: CMs vs. IMs. In principle, CMs are a special class of IMs with a singleton initial state space. Conversely, as described earlier, IMs can be represented by CMs. Both CMs and IMs are defined by a randomized function mapping a state and query message to another state and an answer message, where message is a general concept. The key distinction between CMs and IMs lies in how differential privacy is defined for each.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: By Definition 2.6, the initial state space of IMs contains multiple states, and DP for IMs is defined based on the neighboring initial states. However, by Definition 3.7, the initial state space of CMs is singleton, and a verifier forces an adversary to generate pairs such that the sequences of the first and second elements of the pairs satisfy certain properties. While the adversary chooses these two mechanisms, the identifier selects one of them to forward. Thus, the definition of DP for CMs is based on a bit.

texts.orig: Define ∼ as a neighboring relation on X , where two tuples x = ( x 1 , . . . , x k ) and x ′ = ( x ′ 1 , . . . , x ′ k ) satisfy x ∼ x ′ if and only if x i ∼ i x ′ i for all 1 ≤ i ≤ k . The composition of NIMs N 1 , . . . , N k is said to be ( ϵ, δ )-DP if the non-interactive mechanism Comp[ N 1 , . . . , N k ] is ( ϵ, δ )-DP w.r.t. ∼ (see Definition 2.2).

The composition of NIMs N 1 , . . . , N k is said to be ( ϵ, δ )-DP if the non-interactive mechanism Comp[ N 1 , . . . , N k ] is ( ϵ, δ )-DP w.r.t. ∼ (see Definition 2.2).

texts.orig: In the proofs of this section, we consider Comp[ N 1 , . . . , N k ] not as a non-interactive, but instead as an interactive mechanism with initial state space X that receives x = ( x 1 , . . . , x k ) not as input, but instead as its first message. Immediately after receiving this message it executes Comp[ N 1 , . . . , N k ] on its initial state and returns its output. Upon receiving a second message, this interactive mechanism halts the communication.

Immediately after receiving this message it executes Comp[ N 1 , . . . , N k ] on its initial state and returns its output. Upon receiving a second message, this interactive mechanism halts the communication.

texts.orig: Concurrent Composition of IMs. Vadhan and Wang [20] extended the definition of the composition of NIMs to the concurrent composition of IMs , where an adversary interacts with k interactive mechanisms M 1 , . . . , M k at the same time. In concurrent composition, the adversary generates a message and determines the receiver of this message (one of the M i s) adaptively based on all the previous messages exchanged between the adversary and the mechanisms M 1 , . . . , M k . texts.orig: Vadhan and Wang [20] show that if each IM M i is ϵ i -DP w.r.t ∼ i , and if the composition of NIMs RR ϵ 1 , 0 , . . . , RR ϵ k , 0 is ( ϵ, δ )-DP, then ConComp[ M 1 , . . . , M k ] is also ( ϵ, δ )-DP against an adaptive adversary. Lyu [18] extends this result for ( ϵ i , δ i )-DP IMs.
texts.text: Vadhan and Wang [20] show that if each IM M i is ϵ i -DP w.r.t ∼ i , and if the composition of NIMs RR ϵ 1 , 0 , . . . , RR ϵ k , 0 is ( ϵ, δ )-DP, then ConComp[ M 1 , . . . , M k ] is also ( ϵ, δ )-DP against an adaptive adversary. Lyu [18] extends this result for ( ϵ i , δ i )-DP IMs.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the concurrent composition of IMs, queries asked to each mechanism are chosen adaptively, not only based on that mechanism's previous responses but also using the responses of the other mechanisms up to that point. The results of [20] and [18] show that this concurrency and additional adaptivity do not compromise privacy guarantees. As a result, all ( ϵ, δ )-DP composition theorems for NIMs, such as the basic [6], advanced [9], and optimal [15] composition theorems, can also be applied to the concurrent composition of IMs.
texts.text: In the concurrent composition of IMs, queries asked to each mechanism are chosen adaptively, not only based on that mechanism's previous responses but also using the responses of the other mechanisms up to that point. The results of [20] and [18] show that this concurrency and additional adaptivity do not compromise privacy guarantees. As a result, all ( ϵ, δ )-DP composition theorems for NIMs, such as the basic [6], advanced [9], and optimal [15] composition theorems, can also be applied to the concurrent composition of IMs.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Concurrent Composition of CMs. We extend the definition of the concurrent composition for IMs to the concurrent composition of CMs, where an adversary can create new CMs or ask pairs of queries from the existing ones. Depending on the type of composition, the created mechanisms are constrained, and the sequence of query pairs asked from each CM satisfies certain properties. Based on a secret bit b ∈ { 0 , 1 } , each CM either receives the first element of every query pair asked from it or the second element. In Section 4.1, we generalize ConComp to ExtConComp and give a formal definition for the concurrent composition of CMs.
texts.text: Concurrent Composition of CMs. We extend the definition of the concurrent composition for IMs to the concurrent composition of CMs, where an adversary can create new CMs or ask pairs of queries from the existing ones. Depending on the type of composition, the created mechanisms are constrained, and the sequence of query pairs asked from each CM satisfies certain properties. Based on a secret bit b ∈ { 0 , 1 } , each CM either receives the first element of every query pair asked from it or the second element. In Section 4.1, we generalize ConComp to ExtConComp and give a formal definition for the concurrent composition of CMs.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To extend Lyu's result on the privacy guarantee of the concurrent composition of IMs to the concurrent composition of CMs, we want to use the following intermediate lemma from their paper: For every ( ϵ, δ )-DP interactive mechanism M and any pair of neighboring initial states s and s ′ for M , there exists an IPM P such that for each b ∈ { 0 , 1 } , the IMs P◦ ∗ RR ϵ,δ ( b ) and M ( b ) are equivalent. However, as we discuss in Section 4.2, there are hidden assumptions in the proof of this lemma. Additionally, we show there that the construction of P in [20], for ϵ i -DP IMs, satisfies a property, required for a later proof.
texts.text: To extend Lyu's result on the privacy guarantee of the concurrent composition of IMs to the concurrent composition of CMs, we want to use the following intermediate lemma from their paper: For every ( ϵ, δ )-DP interactive mechanism M and any pair of neighboring initial states s and s ′ for M , there exists an IPM P such that for each b ∈ { 0 , 1 } , the IMs P◦ ∗ RR ϵ,δ ( b ) and M ( b ) are equivalent. However, as we discuss in Section 4.2, there are hidden assumptions in the proof of this lemma. Additionally, we show there that the construction of P in [20], for ϵ i -DP IMs, satisfies a property, required for a later proof.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In Section 4.3, we analyze the privacy of the concurrent composition of k fi xed CMs M 1 , . . . , M k . In this composition, the adversary first creates the mechanisms M 1 , . . . , M k and then asks pairs of queries from each mechanism, ensuring that the sequence of query pairs for each mechanism is valid. In Section 4.4, we extend this composition by fixing k privacy parameters ( ϵ 1 , δ 1 ) , . . . , ( ϵ k , δ k ) and allowing the adversary to adaptively choose the ( ϵ i , δ i )-DP mechanism M i over time.

In Section 4.4, we extend this composition by fixing k privacy parameters ( ϵ 1 , δ 1 ) , . . . , ( ϵ k , δ k ) and allowing the adversary to adaptively choose the ( ϵ i , δ i )-DP mechanism M i over time.

texts.orig: 4.1. Formalization. We extend the definition of ConComp in [20] and introduce ExtConComp that allows the creation of new mechanisms in addition to queries to existing ones. A creation query ( M , s, ϵ, δ, f ) is a message that requests the creation of a continual mechanism M with the initial state s and asserts that M is ( ϵ, δ )-DP w.r.t. the verification function f . The mechanism ExtConComp accepts creation queries and messages of the form ( q, i ), where q is a query for the i -th created mechanism. The formal definition of ExtConComp is as follows:

texts.orig: Definition 4.3 (DP for f -Concurrent Composition of CMs) . For a verification function f and privacy parameter ϵ ≥ 0 and 0 ≤ δ ≤ 1 , the f -concurrent composition of CMs is ( ϵ, δ )-DP against adaptive adversaries if the interactive mechanism V [ f ] ◦ ∗ I◦ ∗ ExtConComp is ( ϵ, δ ) -DP w.r.t. ∼ 01 . Equivalently, this holds if the continual mechanism ExtConComp is ( ϵ, δ ) -DP w.r.t. f against adaptive adversaries.
texts.text: Definition 4.3 (DP for f -Concurrent Composition of CMs) . For a verification function f and privacy parameter ϵ ≥ 0 and 0 ≤ δ ≤ 1 , the f -concurrent composition of CMs is ( ϵ, δ )-DP against adaptive adversaries if the interactive mechanism V [ f ] ◦ ∗ I◦ ∗ ExtConComp is ( ϵ, δ ) -DP w.r.t. ∼ 01 . Equivalently, this holds if the continual mechanism ExtConComp is ( ϵ, δ ) -DP w.r.t. f against adaptive adversaries.

texts.orig: Although verification functions f for ExtConComp vary for different compositions, they must all ensure that the format of the adversary's messages is compatible with I◦ ∗ ExtConComp. An adversary is expected to either create a new CM or send a pair of queries to an existing one. The creation of a new mechanism is independent of the initial state of I . Thus, when requesting the creation of a new mechanism, the adversary must send a pair of identical creation queries to V [ f ]. Moreover, due to the definition of I and ExtConComp, to request asking one of the queries q 0 and q 1 from the i -th created mechanism according to I 's initial state, the adversary must send the message (( q 0 , i ) , ( q 1 , i )) to V [ f ].

texts.orig: Definition 4.4 (Suitable Sequence of Messages) . A sequence of messages m 1 , . . . , m k is suitable if for every 1 ≤ j ≤ k , (i) m j is of the form ( m 0 j , m 1 j ) , where m 0 j and m 1 j are either identical creation queries or messages of the forms ( q 0 , ℓ ) and ( q 1 , ℓ ) with the same index ℓ ; (ii) for each b ∈ { 0 , 1 } , if m b j = ( q, ℓ ) , then there are at least ℓ creation queries in the sequence m b 1 , . . . , m b j -1 ; (iii) for each b ∈ { 0 , 1 } , if m b j = ( q, ℓ ) and M ℓ is the ℓ -th mechanism requested to be created in m b 1 , . . . , m b j -1 , then q is in the query space of M ℓ , i.e., q ∈ Q M ℓ ; and (iv) if m 0 j = m 1 j = ( M , s, ϵ, δ, f ) , then M is a CM with a single initial state s , and it is ( ϵ, δ ) -DP w.r.t. f .
texts.text: Definition 4.4 (Suitable Sequence of Messages) . A sequence of messages m 1 , . . . , m k is suitable if for every 1 ≤ j ≤ k , (i) m j is of the form ( m 0 j , m 1 j ) , where m 0 j and m 1 j are either identical creation queries or messages of the forms ( q 0 , ℓ ) and ( q 1 , ℓ ) with the same index ℓ ; (ii) for each b ∈ { 0 , 1 } , if m b j = ( q, ℓ ) , then there are at least ℓ creation queries in the sequence m b 1 , . . . , m b j -1 ; (iii) for each b ∈ { 0 , 1 } , if m b j = ( q, ℓ ) and M ℓ is the ℓ -th mechanism requested to be created in m b 1 , . . . , m b j -1 , then q is in the query space of M ℓ , i.e., q ∈ Q M ℓ ; and (iv) if m 0 j = m 1 j = ( M , s, ϵ, δ, f ) , then M is a CM with a single initial state s , and it is ( ϵ, δ ) -DP w.r.t. f .

texts.orig: 4.2. ( ϵ, δ ) -DP IMs and RR ϵ,δ . Lyu [18] shows that for every ( ϵ, δ )-DP IM M and every pair of neighboring initial states s 0 and s 1 , there exists an IPM T (with a single initial state) such that for each b ∈ { 0 , 1 } , the mechanisms T ◦ ∗ RR ϵ,δ ( b ) and M ( s b ) are identical. Vadhan and Wang [20] show the same result in the special case where δ = 0.
texts.text: 4.2. ( ϵ, δ ) -DP IMs and RR ϵ,δ . Lyu [18] shows that for every ( ϵ, δ )-DP IM M and every pair of neighboring initial states s 0 and s 1 , there exists an IPM T (with a single initial state) such that for each b ∈ { 0 , 1 } , the mechanisms T ◦ ∗ RR ϵ,δ ( b ) and M ( s b ) are identical. Vadhan and Wang [20] show the same result in the special case where δ = 0.

texts.orig: Definition 4.5. An IM M has discrete answer distributions if A M is countable, and for every initial state s , integer t ∈ N , history ( q 1 , a 1 , . . . , q t -1 , a t -1 ) ∈ ( Q M × A M ) t -1 , and new query q t ∈ Q M , the distribution of M ( s ) 's answer in respond to q t conditioned on having the history ( q 1 , . . . , a t -1 ) has a probability mass function (PMF).
texts.text: Definition 4.5. An IM M has discrete answer distributions if A M is countable, and for every initial state s , integer t ∈ N , history ( q 1 , a 1 , . . . , q t -1 , a t -1 ) ∈ ( Q M × A M ) t -1 , and new query q t ∈ Q M , the distribution of M ( s ) 's answer in respond to q t conditioned on having the history ( q 1 , . . . , a t -1 ) has a probability mass function (PMF).

texts.orig: This is generally not a restrictive assumption in differential privacy. In practice, the binary nature of computers and the fact that the total number of memory bits in the world is finite ensure that every distribution sampled computationally is inherently discrete. Theoretically, when dealing with real-valued outputs, one can enforce discrete answer distributions by rounding each output to the nearest multiple of a sufficiently small constant α . This discretization post-processing preserves the privacy of the mechanism and has a negligible effect on the accuracy. An example of this approach will be provided in Section 7.
texts.text: This is generally not a restrictive assumption in differential privacy. In practice, the binary nature of computers and the fact that the total number of memory bits in the world is finite ensure that every distribution sampled computationally is inherently discrete. Theoretically, when dealing with real-valued outputs, one can enforce discrete answer distributions by rounding each output to the nearest multiple of a sufficiently small constant α . This discretization post-processing preserves the privacy of the mechanism and has a negligible effect on the accuracy. An example of this approach will be provided in Section 7.

texts.orig: Lemma 4.6 ([18]) . For ϵ > 0 and 0 ≤ δ ≤ 1 , let M be a CM that is ( ϵ, δ ) -DP w.r.t. a verification function f . Suppose V [ f ] ◦ ∗ I◦ ∗ M has discrete answer distributions and responds to at most c queries. Then, there exists an IPM T with a single initial state () such that for each b ∈ { 0 , 1 } , the IMs T ◦ ∗ RR ϵ,δ ( b ) and V [ f ] ◦ ∗ I ( b ) ◦ ∗ M are equivalent.

Then, there exists an IPM T with a single initial state () such that for each b ∈ { 0 , 1 } , the IMs T ◦ ∗ RR ϵ,δ ( b ) and V [ f ] ◦ ∗ I ( b ) ◦ ∗ M are equivalent.

texts.orig: Let M be an IM that is ϵ -DP w.r.t. a neighbor relation ∼ . For every pair of neighboring initial states s 0 and s 1 , Vadhan and Wang [20] construct an IPM T (with a single initial state) such that for each bit b ∈ { 0 , 1 } , the mechanisms T ◦ ∗ RR ϵ, 0 ( b ) and M ( s b ) are identical. Appendix B, we will review their construction and show that for any query sequence q 1 , . . . , q t , if the distributions of the t answers of M ( s 0 ) and M ( s 1 ) to the queries q 1 , . . . , q t are identical, then T does not need to know the output of RR ϵ, 0 ( b ) in the first t steps. This observation allows us to delay the interaction of T and RR ϵ, 0 ( b ) as much as possible.

texts.orig: 4.3. Concurrent Composition of CMs M 1 , . . . , M k . For 1 ≤ i ≤ k , let M i be a CM with the initial state space S i = { s i } such that M i is ( ϵ i , δ i )-DP w.r.t. a verification function f i , and V [ f i ] ◦ ∗ I◦ ∗ M i has discrete answer distributions. In the concurrent composition of M 1 , . . . , M k , an adversary is restricted to the following steps: (1) It first creates the fixed mechanisms M 1 , . . . , M k and (2) then adaptively issues pairs of queries for them such that the sequences of query pairs for each M i is f i -valid. Note that creating the CM M i means creating an instance of M i ( s i ), where s i is the unique initial state of M i . Based on a secret bit b ∈ { 0 , 1 } , each CM either receives the first element of every query pair addressed to it or the second element, and the adversary tries to guess b using the answers of all k mechanisms.
texts.text: 4.3. Concurrent Composition of CMs M 1 , . . . , M k . For 1 ≤ i ≤ k , let M i be a CM with the initial state space S i = { s i } such that M i is ( ϵ i , δ i )-DP w.r.t. a verification function f i , and V [ f i ] ◦ ∗ I◦ ∗ M i has discrete answer distributions. In the concurrent composition of M 1 , . . . , M k , an adversary is restricted to the following steps: (1) It first creates the fixed mechanisms M 1 , . . . , M k and (2) then adaptively issues pairs of queries for them such that the sequences of query pairs for each M i is f i -valid. Note that creating the CM M i means creating an instance of M i ( s i ), where s i is the unique initial state of M i . Based on a secret bit b ∈ { 0 , 1 } , each CM either receives the first element of every query pair addressed to it or the second element, and the adversary tries to guess b using the answers of all k mechanisms.

texts.orig: To formalize this type of concurrent composition of CMs, we introduce the verification function f α 1 ,...,α k . Intuitively, this verification function enforces that the adversary creates the mechanisms M 1 , . . . , M k in their first k messages and then only sends messages containing pairs of queries for M i s. Additionally, it ensures that the sequence of query pairs for each M i is f i -valid. The formal definition of f α 1 ,...,α k is as follows:

texts.orig: Recall that in Section 3, we generalized the notion of neighboring relations for interactive mechanisms to verification functions for continual mechanisms. The neighboring initial states for an interactive mechanism are chosen non-adaptively at initialization. However, the choice of messages forming a valid sequence w.r.t. a verification function is adaptive and happens over time. That is, an adversary generates two different query sequences for a CM, and the choice of differing sequences is made adaptively throughout the interaction.
texts.text: Recall that in Section 3, we generalized the notion of neighboring relations for interactive mechanisms to verification functions for continual mechanisms. The neighboring initial states for an interactive mechanism are chosen non-adaptively at initialization. However, the choice of messages forming a valid sequence w.r.t. a verification function is adaptive and happens over time. That is, an adversary generates two different query sequences for a CM, and the choice of differing sequences is made adaptively throughout the interaction.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the concurrent composition of IMs, queries to each mechanism are chosen adaptively, not only based on that mechanism's previous responses but also using the responses of the other mechanisms up to that point. By definition, the choice of neighboring initial states for the interactive mechanisms is non-adaptive . However, in the concurrent composition of CMs, the choice of differences between query sequences, i.e., the neighboring query sequences is adaptive, based on not only the responses of the corresponding mechanism but also the responses of other mechanisms. (Note that, unlike IMs, CMs inherently have a single fixed initial state; hence, no choice of initial state is involved.) Despite this additional adaptivity, in the following theorem, we show that the privacy results of [18] for ConComp[ M 1 , . . . , M k ] also hold for V [ f α 1 ,...,α k ] ◦ ∗ I◦ ∗ ExtConComp.
texts.text: In the concurrent composition of IMs, queries to each mechanism are chosen adaptively, not only based on that mechanism's previous responses but also using the responses of the other mechanisms up to that point. By definition, the choice of neighboring initial states for the interactive mechanisms is non-adaptive . However, in the concurrent composition of CMs, the choice of differences between query sequences, i.e., the neighboring query sequences is adaptive, based on not only the responses of the corresponding mechanism but also the responses of other mechanisms. (Note that, unlike IMs, CMs inherently have a single fixed initial state; hence, no choice of initial state is involved.) Despite this additional adaptivity, in the following theorem, we show that the privacy results of [18] for ConComp[ M 1 , . . . , M k ] also hold for V [ f α 1 ,...,α k ] ◦ ∗ I◦ ∗ ExtConComp.

texts.orig: Theorem 4.10. For 1 ≤ i ≤ k , let M i be a continual mechanism with initial state space S init M i = { s i } such that M i is ( ϵ i , δ i ) -DP w.r.t. a verification function f i against adaptive adversaries, and V [ f i ] ◦ ∗ I◦ ∗ M i has discrete answer distributions. For ϵ > 0 and 0 ≤ δ ≤ 1 , if the composition of non-interactive mechanisms RR ϵ 1 ,δ 1 , . . . , RR ϵ k ,δ k is ( ϵ, δ ) -DP, then the concurrent composition of M 1 , . . . , M k is also ( ϵ, δ ) -DP against adaptive adversaries.
texts.text: Theorem 4.10. For 1 ≤ i ≤ k , let M i be a continual mechanism with initial state space S init M i = { s i } such that M i is ( ϵ i , δ i ) -DP w.r.t. a verification function f i against adaptive adversaries, and V [ f i ] ◦ ∗ I◦ ∗ M i has discrete answer distributions. For ϵ > 0 and 0 ≤ δ ≤ 1 , if the composition of non-interactive mechanisms RR ϵ 1 ,δ 1 , . . . , RR ϵ k ,δ k is ( ϵ, δ ) -DP, then the concurrent composition of M 1 , . . . , M k is also ( ϵ, δ ) -DP against adaptive adversaries.

texts.orig: Proof. Let α i = ( M i , s i , ϵ i , δ i , f i ) for each i ∈ [ k ], and let f denote f α 1 ,...,α k for simplicity. Our goal is to show that ExtConComp is ( ϵ, δ )-DP w.r.t. f . To prove so, we construct an IPM P with a single initial state and show that for each b ∈ { 0 , 1 } , the mechanisms V [ f ] ◦ ∗ I ( b ) ◦ ∗ ExtConComp and V [ f ] ◦ ∗ P◦ ∗ Comp[RR ϵ 1 ,δ 1 , . . . , RR ϵ k ,δ k ] ( ( b ) k i =1 ) are equivalent.

To prove so, we construct an IPM P with a single initial state and show that for each b ∈ { 0 , 1 } , the mechanisms V [ f ] ◦ ∗ I ( b ) ◦ ∗ ExtConComp and V [ f ] ◦ ∗ P◦ ∗ Comp[RR ϵ 1 ,δ 1 , . . . , RR ϵ k ,δ k ] ( ( b ) k i =1 ) are equivalent.

texts.orig: Since V [ f ] ◦ ∗ P is an IPM with a single initial state, Lemma 2.15 implies that if Comp[RR ϵ 1 ,δ 1 , . . . , RR ϵ k ,δ k ] is ( ϵ, δ )-DP, then V [ f ] ◦ ∗ P◦ ∗ Comp[RR ϵ 1 ,δ 1 , . . . , RR ϵ k ,δ k ] is also ( ϵ, δ )-DP. Hence, for any adversary A , the views of A when interacting with the mechanisms V [ f ] ◦ ∗ P◦ ∗ Comp[RR ϵ 1 ,δ 1 , . . . , RR ϵ k ,δ k ] ( (0) k i =1 ) and V [ f ] ◦ ∗ P◦ ∗ Comp[RR ϵ 1 ,δ 1 , . . . , RR ϵ k ,δ k ] ( (1) k i =1 ) are ( ϵ, δ )-indistinguishable. As we will show below, by construction, these views are identical to those obtained by interacting with the original mechanisms V [ f ] ◦ ∗ I ( b ) ◦ ∗ ExtConComp, implying that the concurrent composition of M 1 , . . . , M k is ( ϵ, δ )-DP.
texts.text: Since V [ f ] ◦ ∗ P is an IPM with a single initial state, Lemma 2.15 implies that if Comp[RR ϵ 1 ,δ 1 , . . . , RR ϵ k ,δ k ] is ( ϵ, δ )-DP, then V [ f ] ◦ ∗ P◦ ∗ Comp[RR ϵ 1 ,δ 1 , . . . , RR ϵ k ,δ k ] is also ( ϵ, δ )-DP. Hence, for any adversary A , the views of A when interacting with the mechanisms V [ f ] ◦ ∗ P◦ ∗ Comp[RR ϵ 1 ,δ 1 , . . . , RR ϵ k ,δ k ] ( (0) k i =1 ) and V [ f ] ◦ ∗ P◦ ∗ Comp[RR ϵ 1 ,δ 1 , . . . , RR ϵ k ,δ k ] ( (1) k i =1 ) are ( ϵ, δ )-indistinguishable. As we will show below, by construction, these views are identical to those obtained by interacting with the original mechanisms V [ f ] ◦ ∗ I ( b ) ◦ ∗ ExtConComp, implying that the concurrent composition of M 1 , . . . , M k is ( ϵ, δ )-DP.

Moreover, upon receiving a left message (( q 0 , i ) , ( q 1 , i )), P sends ( q 0 , q 1 ) as a left message to T i and returns its response. If T i requests a sample from RR ϵ i ,δ i , P provides it with the previously stored sample r i . texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 4.4. Concurrent Composition of CMs with Fixed Parameters. The concurrent composition of CMs with fixed parameters generalizes the concurrent composition of fixed CMs by allowing the adversary to select the CMs M 1 , . . . , M k adaptively over time, instead of forcing them to create a fixed set of mechanisms in their first k messages. Each selected mechanism M i must be ( ϵ i , δ i )-DP w.r.t. a verification function f i , where the multiset of privacy parameters ( ϵ 1 , δ 1 ) , . . . , ( ϵ k , δ k ) is fixed in advance, but the verification functions f i are chosen by the adversary. Note that this implies that at most k continual mechanism are created by the adversary. Moreover, due to the aforementioned technical reasons, each V [ f i ] ◦ ∗ I◦ ∗ M i must have discrete answer distributions.
texts.text: 4.4. Concurrent Composition of CMs with Fixed Parameters. The concurrent composition of CMs with fixed parameters generalizes the concurrent composition of fixed CMs by allowing the adversary to select the CMs M 1 , . . . , M k adaptively over time, instead of forcing them to create a fixed set of mechanisms in their first k messages. Each selected mechanism M i must be ( ϵ i , δ i )-DP w.r.t. a verification function f i , where the multiset of privacy parameters ( ϵ 1 , δ 1 ) , . . . , ( ϵ k , δ k ) is fixed in advance, but the verification functions f i are chosen by the adversary. Note that this implies that at most k continual mechanism are created by the adversary. Moreover, due to the aforementioned technical reasons, each V [ f i ] ◦ ∗ I◦ ∗ M i must have discrete answer distributions.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For instance, an adversary may first create a continual mechanism M 3 that is ( ϵ 3 , δ 3 )-DP w.r.t. a verification function f 3 . Then, after interacting with M 3 for some time and analyzing its responses, the adversary may then select a mechanism M 1 from the class of ( ϵ 1 , δ 1 )-DP CMs. Similar to the concurrent composition of fixed CMs, the adversary's sequence of query pairs for each M i must be f i -valid.

When T j requests a sample from RR ϵ ′ j ,δ ′ j , P iterates over σ 1 to find the smallest i ∗ ∈ [ k ] such that the i ∗ -th element of σ 1 is not null and ϵ ′ j = ϵ i ∗ , δ ′ j = δ i ∗ . After sending r i ∗ to T j , P updates σ 1 and sets r i to null. This adjustment ensures that even when there are duplicate privacy parameters among ( ϵ 1 , δ 1 ) , . . . , ( ϵ k , δ k ), each sample r i is sent to at most one T j . Note that by the definition of f ϵ 1 ,δ 1 ,...,ϵ k ,δ k , i ∗ always exists. □

texts.orig: 5. Concurrent Parallel Composition of CMs
texts.text: 5. Concurrent Parallel Composition of CMs

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The concurrent k -sparse parallel composition extends the concurrent composition of CMs with k fixed parameters by permitting the creation of an arbitrary number of CMs. Specifically, given a multiset params of k privacy parameters ( ϵ 1 , δ 1 ) , . . . , ( ϵ k , δ k ), the adversary can create an unlimited number of CMs with arbitrary privacy parameters. However, the adversary must ask pairs of identical queries to all created mechanisms, except for at most k of them. Let M 1 , . . . , M k be these exceptional mechanisms. Each M i , selected by the adversary, must be ( ϵ i , δ i )-DP w.r.t. a verification function f i and with ( ϵ i , δ i ) ∈ params , and the sequence of query pairs for M i must be f i -valid.
texts.text: The concurrent k -sparse parallel composition extends the concurrent composition of CMs with k fixed parameters by permitting the creation of an arbitrary number of CMs. Specifically, given a multiset params of k privacy parameters ( ϵ 1 , δ 1 ) , . . . , ( ϵ k , δ k ), the adversary can create an unlimited number of CMs with arbitrary privacy parameters. However, the adversary must ask pairs of identical queries to all created mechanisms, except for at most k of them. Let M 1 , . . . , M k be these exceptional mechanisms. Each M i , selected by the adversary, must be ( ϵ i , δ i )-DP w.r.t. a verification function f i and with ( ϵ i , δ i ) ∈ params , and the sequence of query pairs for M i must be f i -valid.

texts.orig: 5.2. Counter Example. In this section, we illustrate that extending the result of Theorem 5.2 to general δ j > 0 is impossible. Specifically, let k = 1, ϵ 1 = 0, and δ 1 = δ ∈ (0 , 1]. We construct, an adversary A δ that interacts with V [ f 0 ,δ ] ◦ ∗ I ( b ) ◦ ∗ ExtConComp and successfully determines the secret bit b ∈ { 0 , 1 } . Our construction proceeds in three steps: (1) introducing a verification function g , (2) constructing a CM M δ that is (0 , δ )-DP w.r.t. g , and (3) designing A δ .

texts.orig: 5.3. Composition Theorem for Restricted Verification Functions. In Section 5.2, we showed that for any positive δ , the concurrent 1-sparse parallel composition of CMs with the privacy parameter (0 , δ ) fails to preserve privacy. In our counterexample, the adversary leveraged the first outcome of a (0 , δ )-DP CM to decide whether to spend its budget on issuing non-identical queries to that mechanism. Intuitively, when δ > 0, there might exist certain 'bad first answers' that occur with low probability; however, once we condition on their occurrences, the distribution of the second answer ceases to be differentially private.
texts.text: 5.3. Composition Theorem for Restricted Verification Functions. In Section 5.2, we showed that for any positive δ , the concurrent 1-sparse parallel composition of CMs with the privacy parameter (0 , δ ) fails to preserve privacy. In our counterexample, the adversary leveraged the first outcome of a (0 , δ )-DP CM to decide whether to spend its budget on issuing non-identical queries to that mechanism. Intuitively, when δ > 0, there might exist certain 'bad first answers' that occur with low probability; however, once we condition on their occurrences, the distribution of the second answer ceases to be differentially private.

texts.orig: In this section, we force the adversary to decide whether it will always issue pairs of identical queries to a CM when it sends the first message to that mechanism. Specifically, if the first message sent to a CM includes identical queries, then all subsequent messages for that CM must also have identical queries. This leads to the following definition.
texts.text: In this section, we force the adversary to decide whether it will always issue pairs of identical queries to a CM when it sends the first message to that mechanism. Specifically, if the first message sent to a CM includes identical queries, then all subsequent messages for that CM must also have identical queries. This leads to the following definition.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: Definition 5.3 (First-Pair Consistent) . A verification function f is first-pair consistent if the following holds: given an input message sequence m 1 , . . . , m t , f returns ⊥ if m 1 is a pair of identical messages, i.e., m 1 = ( m ′ , m ′ ) for some m ′ , while there exists 2 ≤ j ≤ t such that m j is not a pair of identical messages.
texts.text: Definition 5.3 (First-Pair Consistent) . A verification function f is first-pair consistent if the following holds: given an input message sequence m 1 , . . . , m t , f returns ⊥ if m 1 is a pair of identical messages, i.e., m 1 = ( m ′ , m ′ ) for some m ′ , while there exists 2 ≤ j ≤ t such that m j is not a pair of identical messages.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For instance, let M be an IM that is ( ϵ, δ )-DP w.r.t. a neighbor relation ∼ . As discussed in Section 3.1, M can be represented by a CM M ′ (with initial state null) that receives an initial state s ∈ S init M as the first message and behaves the same as M ( s ) afterwards. Define the verification function f ∼ as follows: given an input message sequence ( m 1 , . . . , m t ), f ∼ returns ⊤ if and only if m 1 is a pair of neighboring initial states in S init M , and for all 2 ≤ j ≤ t , m j is a pair of identical queries in Q M . By definition, f ∼ is first-pair consistent.
texts.text: For instance, let M be an IM that is ( ϵ, δ )-DP w.r.t. a neighbor relation ∼ . As discussed in Section 3.1, M can be represented by a CM M ′ (with initial state null) that receives an initial state s ∈ S init M as the first message and behaves the same as M ( s ) afterwards. Define the verification function f ∼ as follows: given an input message sequence ( m 1 , . . . , m t ), f ∼ returns ⊤ if and only if m 1 is a pair of neighboring initial states in S init M , and for all 2 ≤ j ≤ t , m j is a pair of identical queries in Q M . By definition, f ∼ is first-pair consistent.

texts.orig: 5.3.1. Concurrent k -sparse Parallel Composition of IM. Consider a setting where an adversary creates (an unlimited number of) IMs over time while adaptively asking queries from the existing ones. When creating an IM M i that is ( ϵ i , δ i )-DP w.r.t. a neighbor relation ∼ i , the adversary also selects a pair of neighboring initial states for M i . This selection depends on the history of queries asked so far and their responses. Based on a secret bit b , either all created mechanisms are initialized with the first initial state in their respective pairs, or all are initialized with the second. The goal of the adversary is to guess b .
texts.text: 5.3.1. Concurrent k -sparse Parallel Composition of IM. Consider a setting where an adversary creates (an unlimited number of) IMs over time while adaptively asking queries from the existing ones. When creating an IM M i that is ( ϵ i , δ i )-DP w.r.t. a neighbor relation ∼ i , the adversary also selects a pair of neighboring initial states for M i . This selection depends on the history of queries asked so far and their responses. Based on a secret bit b , either all created mechanisms are initialized with the first initial state in their respective pairs, or all are initialized with the second. The goal of the adversary is to guess b .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This scenario is an special case of FPC concurrent k -sparse parallel composition of CMs, where, instead of creating M i and assigning a pair of initial states to it, the adversary sends ( M ′ i , null , ϵ i , δ i , f ∼ i ) 2 , where M ′ i is the CM simulating M i . Moreover, to ask a query q from the i -th created IM, the adversary sends (( q, i ) , ( q, i )). Since each f ∼ i is first-pair consistent, Theorem 5.5 applies to the concurrent k -sparse parallel composition of IM with privacy parameters (( ϵ i , δ i ) k i =1 (that are differentially private w.r.t. neighbor relations).

Since each f ∼ i is first-pair consistent, Theorem 5.5 applies to the concurrent k -sparse parallel composition of IM with privacy parameters (( ϵ i , δ i ) k i =1 (that are differentially private w.r.t. neighbor relations).

Comparison with the Parallel Composition of NIMs. Consider a set of ℓ NIMs, N 1 , . . . , N ℓ , where each N i has a domain X i and is ( ϵ i , δ i )-DP w.r.t. a neighbor relation ∼ i . The k -sparse parallel composition of NIMs N 1 , . . . , N ℓ is said to be ( ϵ, δ )-DP if the non-interactive mechanism Comp( N 1 , . . . , N ℓ ) with the domain X = X 1 ×···×X ℓ is ( ϵ, δ )-DP w.r.t. the neighbor relation ∼ par , defined as follows: for any two x = ( x 1 , . . . x ℓ ) and x ′ = ( x ′ 1 , . . . x ′ ℓ ) in X , we have x ∼ par x ′ if and only if there exists a subset J ⊆ { 1 , . . . , ℓ } of size at most k such that x j ∼ j x ′ j for every j ∈ J , and x j = x ′ j for every j ∈ { 1 , . . . , ℓ } \ J .

The k -sparse parallel composition of NIMs N 1 , . . . , N ℓ is said to be ( ϵ, δ )-DP if the non-interactive mechanism Comp( N 1 , . . . , N ℓ ) with the domain X = X 1 ×···×X ℓ is ( ϵ, δ )-DP w.r.t. the neighbor relation ∼ par , defined as follows: for any two x = ( x 1 , . . . x ℓ ) and x ′ = ( x ′ 1 , . . . x ′ ℓ ) in X , we have x ∼ par x ′ if and only if there exists a subset J ⊆ { 1 , . . . , ℓ } of size at most k such that x j ∼ j x ′ j for every j ∈ J , and x j = x ′ j for every j ∈ { 1 , . . . , ℓ } \ J .

texts.orig: The concurrent parallel composition of IMs generalizes the parallel composition of NIMs in several key ways: Rather than composing a fixed set of ℓ NIMs, an adversary adaptively selects an unbounded number of IMs with arbitrary privacy parameters. Additionally, instead of non-adaptively selecting worst-case neighboring initial states for (at most) k mechanisms and identical initial states for the other ℓ -k mechanisms, the adversary adaptively (1) chooses pairs of initial states based on prior interactions with other mechanisms, while (2) ensuring that the initial states in at most k pairs are neighboring, and that they are identical in the other pairs. In the concurrent parallel composition of CMs, the adversary is even stronger: instead of choosing a single pair of neighboring initial states, it adaptively selects a sequence of pairs , satisfying the constraints enforced by a verification function.
texts.text: The concurrent parallel composition of IMs generalizes the parallel composition of NIMs in several key ways: Rather than composing a fixed set of ℓ NIMs, an adversary adaptively selects an unbounded number of IMs with arbitrary privacy parameters. Additionally, instead of non-adaptively selecting worst-case neighboring initial states for (at most) k mechanisms and identical initial states for the other ℓ -k mechanisms, the adversary adaptively (1) chooses pairs of initial states based on prior interactions with other mechanisms, while (2) ensuring that the initial states in at most k pairs are neighboring, and that they are identical in the other pairs. In the concurrent parallel composition of CMs, the adversary is even stronger: instead of choosing a single pair of neighboring initial states, it adaptively selects a sequence of pairs , satisfying the constraints enforced by a verification function.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The 1-sparse parallel composition of NIMs is commonly used when a dataset is partitioned among ℓ NIMs such that for any two neighboring datasets differing by a single record, all partitions remain identical except for the presence of the differing record in one partition. Consider the case where an adversary creates CMs receiving data or empty records as query messages. In the concurrent parallel composition of CMs, the adversary adaptively determines the number of partitions, assigns records to them, and selects which partitions contain differing sets of records.

texts.orig: It is known that the k -sparse parallel composition of ℓ ( ϵ 0 , δ 0 )-DP NIMs is ( ϵ, δ )-DP if the composition of ℓ instances of RR ϵ 0 ,δ 0 is ( ϵ, δ )-DP. We showed that any NIM that is ( ϵ 0 , δ 0 )-DP w.r.t. a neighbor relation ∼ can be modeled by a CM that is ( ϵ 0 , δ 0 )-DP w.r.t. f ∼ , which is a first-pair consistent verification function. Thus, setting ϵ 1 = · · · = ϵ k = ϵ 0 , δ 1 = · · · = δ k = δ 0 in Theorem 5.5 recovers and generalizes the classic parallel composition theorem. Our theorem extends differential privacy guarantees to settings where an adversary can adaptively create an unbounded number of NIMs.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: So far we discussed the setting where the messages are issued by an adversary. However, in the design of differentially private mechanisms in the streaming setting, it is common to use existing differentially private interactive mechanisms, such as the Sparse Vector Technique (SVT) and continual counters, as sub-mechanisms. Thus, it is the main mechanism M that issues messages to its sub-mechanisms. The main mechanism may fix the set of sub-mechanisms at initialization or select these mechanisms adaptively over time. For example, in Section 7, we present a mechanism that maintains d fi xed binary counters while adaptively instantiating an unbounded number of SVT and Laplace mechanisms as required.

For example, in Section 7, we present a mechanism that maintains d fi xed binary counters while adaptively instantiating an unbounded number of SVT and Laplace mechanisms as required.

texts.orig: The privacy analysis of such mechanisms M is challenging as the creation of sub-mechanisms and their inputs is adaptive and dependent. The goal of this section is to give a set of conditions for M and prove a theorem that shows that under these conditions the privacy of M can directly be computed by applying the known concurrent composition theorems for continual mechanisms to the sub-mechanisms. Specifically, given that the f ′ -concurrent composition of CMs is ( ϵ, δ )-DP w.r.t. some verification function f ′ , we would like to show that M is ( ϵ, δ )-DP w.r.t. the given verification function f .

texts.orig: To prove that M is ( ϵ, δ )-DP w.r.t. f , we need to show that the view of every adversary A interacting with V [ f ] ◦ ∗ I (0) ◦ ∗ M = V [ f ] ◦ ∗ I (0) ◦ ∗ P◦ ∗ ExtConComp and V [ f ] ◦ ∗ I (1) ◦ ∗ M = V [ f ] ◦ ∗ I (1) ◦ ∗ P◦ ∗ ExtConComp is ( ϵ, δ )-indistinguishable. Intuitively, the messages that P◦ ∗ ExtConComp receives from I ( b ) contains information about b . Therefore, to deduce that P◦ ∗ ExtConComp is ( ϵ, δ )-DP w.r.t. f from the fact that ExtConComp is ( ϵ, δ )-DP w.r.t. f ′ , the IPM P must not directly use the messages from I to respond. Instead, it should only use these messages to determine which queries it wants to send to its its sub-mechanisms via ExtConComp. More precisely, P must generate an answer for I only based on the privatized answers provided by ExtConComp and not dependent on the un-privatized input sequence (Response Property). Moreover, P must decide whether to continue interacting with ExtConComp or to return a response based on these privatized answers (Destination Property).

texts.orig: 6.1. Properties. To formally state that P maps f -valid sequences to f ′ -valid ones, we assume that P is deterministic . This means P can be expressed as a deterministic function that maps the sequence of received messages to its next output message. This assumption is not restrictive, as randomness can be modeled by a 0-DP sub-mechanism M i that accepts a single message ⊤ , and upon receiving it, generates a sufficiently long sequence of random bits or samples from a fixed distribution.
texts.text: 6.1. Properties. To formally state that P maps f -valid sequences to f ′ -valid ones, we assume that P is deterministic . This means P can be expressed as a deterministic function that maps the sequence of received messages to its next output message. This assumption is not restrictive, as randomness can be modeled by a 0-DP sub-mechanism M i that accepts a single message ⊤ , and upon receiving it, generates a sufficiently long sequence of random bits or samples from a fixed distribution.

texts.orig: · Destination Property. The destination L or R of messages sent by P is determined solely based on messages received from its right mechanism (here, ExtConComp). This ensures that if two transcripts contain identical sequences of messages from the right mechanism but different sequences from the left mechanism, P will still interact with the same mechanism in the next round.
texts.text: Destination Property. The destination L or R of messages sent by P is determined solely based on messages received from its right mechanism (here, ExtConComp). This ensures that if two transcripts contain identical sequences of messages from the right mechanism but different sequences from the left mechanism, P will still interact with the same mechanism in the next round.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Response Property. The responses sent by P to the left mechanism depend only on the messages received from the right mechanism. That is, if two transcripts contain identical sequences of messages from the right mechanism and P sends its next message to the left mechanism, then this message is identical in both cases.

texts.orig: Theorem 6.1. Let P be a deterministic interactive post-processing mechanism with a singleton initial state space satisfying the destination, response, and f → f ′ properties. If ExtConComp is ( ϵ, δ ) -DP w.r.t. f ′ against adaptive adversaries, then the continual mechanism P◦ ∗ ExtConComp is ( ϵ, δ ) -DP w.r.t. f against adaptive adversaries.
texts.text: Theorem 6.1. Let P be a deterministic interactive post-processing mechanism with a singleton initial state space satisfying the destination, response, and f → f ′ properties. The goal is to show the same result for V [ f ] ◦ ∗ I (0) ◦ ∗ P◦ ∗ ExtConComp and V [ f ] ◦ ∗ I (1) ◦ ∗ P◦ ∗ ExtConComp. The key idea of the proof is to show that the IMs V [ f ] ◦ ∗ I ( b ) ◦ ∗ P◦ ∗ ExtConComp and T ◦ ∗ V [ f ′ ] ◦ ∗ I ( b ) ◦ ∗ ExtConComp are identical, where T is an IPM with a singleton initial state space. The proof consists of two steps.
texts.text: In Theorem 6.1, we assume that the view of any adversary interacting with V [ f ′ ] ◦ ∗ I (0) ◦ ∗ ExtConComp and V [ f ′ ] ◦ ∗ I (1) ◦ ∗ ExtConComp is ( ϵ, δ )-indistinguishable. The goal is to show the same result for V [ f ] ◦ ∗ I (0) ◦ ∗ P◦ ∗ ExtConComp and V [ f ] ◦ ∗ I (1) ◦ ∗ P◦ ∗ ExtConComp. The key idea of the proof is to show that the IMs V [ f ] ◦ ∗ I ( b ) ◦ ∗ P◦ ∗ ExtConComp and T ◦ ∗ V [ f ′ ] ◦ ∗ I ( b ) ◦ ∗ ExtConComp are identical, where T is an IPM with a singleton initial state space. The proof consists of two steps.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Step 1 (Swapping P and I ( b )). We start by swapping the positions of P and I ( b ) in V [ f ] ◦ ∗ I ( b ) ◦ ∗ P◦ ∗ ExtConComp. The verifier V [ f ] sends pairs of query messages, while P expects a single one. Also, the identifier I ( b ) expects pairs of messages, while P generates a single response. Therefore, when swapping P and I ( b ), we should replace P◦ ∗ I ( b ) with I ( b ) ◦ ∗ P 2 , where P 2 is an interactive post-processing mechanism that runs two instances of P in parallel. The right mechanism of P 2 is I◦ ∗ ExtConComp, and its left mechanism is V [ f ]. The exact definition of P 2 is given below.
texts.text: Step 1 (Swapping P and I ( b )). We start by swapping the positions of P and I ( b ) in V [ f ] ◦ ∗ I ( b ) ◦ ∗ P◦ ∗ ExtConComp. The verifier V [ f ] sends pairs of query messages, while P expects a single one. Also, the identifier I ( b ) expects pairs of messages, while P generates a single response. Therefore, when swapping P and I ( b ), we should replace P◦ ∗ I ( b ) with I ( b ) ◦ ∗ P 2 , where P 2 is an interactive post-processing mechanism that runs two instances of P in parallel. The right mechanism of P 2 is I◦ ∗ ExtConComp, and its left mechanism is V [ f ]. The exact definition of P 2 is given below.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Definition 6.2 ( P 2 ) . Let P : S P × ( ( { L } × Q L P ) ∪ ( { R } × Q R P ) ) → S P × ( ( { L } × A L P ) ∪ ( { R } × A R P ) ) be a deterministic interactive post-processing mechanism with a singleton initial state space S init P = { s init } , satisfying the destination and response properties. P 2 : S 2 P × ( ( { L } × Q L P ) ∪ ( { R } × Q R P × Q R P ) ) → S P × ( ( { L } × A L P ) ∪ ( { R } × A R P ) ) is an interactive postprocessing mechanism that executes two instances of P , referred to as P 0 and P 1 . The state of P 2 is a pair ( s 0 , s 1 ) denoting the current states of P 0 and P 1 . The initial state space of P 2 is { ( s init , s init ) } .
texts.text: Definition 6.2 ( P 2 ) . Let P : S P × ( ( { L } × Q L P ) ∪ ( { R } × Q R P ) ) → S P × ( ( { L } × A L P ) ∪ ( { R } × A R P ) ) be a deterministic interactive post-processing mechanism with a singleton initial state space S init P = { s init } , satisfying the destination and response properties. P 2 : S 2 P × ( ( { L } × Q L P ) ∪ ( { R } × Q R P × Q R P ) ) → S P × ( ( { L } × A L P ) ∪ ( { R } × A R P ) ) is an interactive postprocessing mechanism that executes two instances of P , referred to as P 0 and P 1 . The state of P 2 is a pair ( s 0 , s 1 ) denoting the current states of P 0 and P 1 . The initial state space of P 2 is { ( s init , s init ) } .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: When P 2 receives a message m ∈ Q L P from the left mechanism, it forwards two copies of m to both P 0 and P 1 as left messages. Specifically, it executes ( s 0 , v 0 , m ′ 0 ) ←P 0 ( s 0 , L , m ) and ( s 1 , v 1 , m ′ 1 ) ← P 1 ( s 1 , L , m ) . When P 2 receives a pair of messages ( m 0 , m 1 ) ∈ Q R P × Q R P from the right mechanism, it passes each m b to P b as a right message. Specifically, it executes ( s 0 , v 0 , m ′ 0 ) ←P 0 ( s 0 , R , m 0 ) and ( s 1 , v 1 , m ′ 1 ) ← P 1 ( s 1 , R , m 0 ) . By the destination property of P and the fact that both P 0 and P 1 receive identical left messages, we have v ′ 0 = v ′ 1 . If v ′ 0 = v ′ 1 = R , then P 2 sends ( m ′ 0 , m ′ 1 ) to its right mechanism. If v ′ 0 = v ′ 1 = L , then by the response property, m ′ 0 = m ′ 1 , and P 2 returns m ′ 0 to its left mechanism.
texts.text: When P 2 receives a message m ∈ Q L P from the left mechanism, it forwards two copies of m to both P 0 and P 1 as left messages. Specifically, it executes ( s 0 , v 0 , m ′ 0 ) ←P 0 ( s 0 , L , m ) and ( s 1 , v 1 , m ′ 1 ) ← P 1 ( s 1 , L , m ) . When P 2 receives a pair of messages ( m 0 , m 1 ) ∈ Q R P × Q R P from the right mechanism, it passes each m b to P b as a right message. Specifically, it executes ( s 0 , v 0 , m ′ 0 ) ←P 0 ( s 0 , R , m 0 ) and ( s 1 , v 1 , m ′ 1 ) ← P 1 ( s 1 , R , m 0 ) . By the destination property of P and the fact that both P 0 and P 1 receive identical left messages, we have v ′ 0 = v ′ 1 . If v ′ 0 = v ′ 1 = R , then P 2 sends ( m ′ 0 , m ′ 1 ) to its right mechanism. If v ′ 0 = v ′ 1 = L , then by the response property, m ′ 0 = m ′ 1 , and P 2 returns m ′ 0 to its left mechanism.

texts.orig: We next show an application of our technique to the continual monotone histogram query problem. The continual monotone histogram query problem is defined as follows.
texts.text: We next show an application of our technique to the continual monotone histogram query problem. The continual monotone histogram query problem is defined as follows.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Definition 7.1. Let d ∈ N and let q : N d → N be a function that is monotone in its input. The input is a finite stream of row vectors x 1 , . . . , x T with x t ∈ { 0 , 1 } d for all t ∈ [ T ] . Let ∼ be a neighbor relation on ( { 0 , 1 } d ) ∗ where two input streams are neighboring if they are identical except for a single step. The output of the (differentially private) continual monotone histogram query problem at each time step t is (an additive approximation to) q ( t ) = q (( ∑ t ℓ =1 x ℓ i ) i ∈ [ d ] ) .
texts.text: Definition 7.1. Let d ∈ N and let q : N d → N be a function that is monotone in its input. The input is a finite stream of row vectors x 1 , . . . , x T with x t ∈ { 0 , 1 } d for all t ∈ [ T ] . Let ∼ be a neighbor relation on ( { 0 , 1 } d ) ∗ where two input streams are neighboring if they are identical except for a single step. The output of the (differentially private) continual monotone histogram query problem at each time step t is (an additive approximation to) q ( t ) = q (( ∑ t ℓ =1 x ℓ i ) i ∈ [ d ] ) .

texts.orig: Definition 7.2. The function q : { 0 , 1 } d → R is 1-sensitive if max x ∼ y | q ( x ) -q ( y ) | is at most 1 .
texts.text: Definition 7.2. The function q : { 0 , 1 } d → R is 1-sensitive if max x ∼ y | q ( x ) -q ( y ) | is at most 1 .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Henzinger, Sricharan, and Steiner [12] presented the best known differentially private mechanism for the monotone histogram query problem, where where the query is 1-sensitive. For ϵ -differential privacy it has, with probability at least 1 -β , an additive error of O (( d log 2 ( dq ∗ /β ) + log T ) ϵ -1 ), and for ( ϵ, δ )-differential privacy, it has an additive error of O (( d log 1 . 5 ( dq ∗ /β ) + log T ) ϵ -1 log 1 δ ), where q ∗ = max t ∈ [ T ] q ( t ). The privacy of the mechanism is proven 'from scratch' using the concept of a privacy game [13] and the latter bound only holds against an oblivious adversary.
texts.text: Henzinger, Sricharan, and Steiner [12] presented the best known differentially private mechanism for the monotone histogram query problem, where where the query is 1-sensitive. For ϵ -differential privacy it has, with probability at least 1 -β , an additive error of O (( d log 2 ( dq ∗ /β ) + log T ) ϵ -1 ), and for ( ϵ, δ )-differential privacy, it has an additive error of O (( d log 1 . 5 ( dq ∗ /β ) + log T ) ϵ -1 log 1 δ ), where q ∗ = max t ∈ [ T ] q ( t ). The privacy of the mechanism is proven 'from scratch' using the concept of a privacy game [13] and the latter bound only holds against an oblivious adversary.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In Section 7.1, we give an overview of the mechanism in [12], which we will call HSS , and in Section 7.2, we give a simple privacy proof for it using our approach. With the new privacy analysis, the result for ( ϵ, δ )-differential privacy is stronger and holds against an adaptive adversary.
texts.text: In Section 7.1, we give an overview of the mechanism in [12], which we will call HSS , and in Section 7.2, we give a simple privacy proof for it using our approach. With the new privacy analysis, the result for ( ϵ, δ )-differential privacy is stronger and holds against an adaptive adversary.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 7.1. Overview of Mechanism. The mechanism HSS , described in Algorithm 4, employs a variant of the Sparse Vector Technique (SVT) defined by two operations. The first, SVT-init ( ϵ, q, h ), initializes the mechanism with the privacy parameter ϵ , the histogram query q , and an initial d -dimensional vector h . The second operation, SVT-call ( x ( t ) , Thresh ), processes an input value x ( t ) ∈ { 0 , 1 } d along with a threshold Thresh . This algorithm maintains a running sum of all the first parameters from SVT-call and tests in a private manner whether the function q applied to the running sum returns a value that is at least Thresh . If so the algorithm returns ⊤ and does not accept any further calls. Otherwise it returns ⊥ and continues to accept calls. These two algorithms are given in detail in Algorithm 3. The command Lap( b ) means that a random value from the Laplacian distribution with parameter b is returned.
texts.text: 7.1. Overview of Mechanism. The mechanism HSS , described in Algorithm 4, employs a variant of the Sparse Vector Technique (SVT) defined by two operations. The first, SVT-init ( ϵ, q, h ), initializes the mechanism with the privacy parameter ϵ , the histogram query q , and an initial d -dimensional vector h . The second operation, SVT-call ( x ( t ) , Thresh ), processes an input value x ( t ) ∈ { 0 , 1 } d along with a threshold Thresh . This algorithm maintains a running sum of all the first parameters from SVT-call and tests in a private manner whether the function q applied to the running sum returns a value that is at least Thresh . If so the algorithm returns ⊤ and does not accept any further calls. Otherwise it returns ⊥ and continues to accept calls. These two algorithms are given in detail in Algorithm 3. The command Lap( b ) means that a random value from the Laplacian distribution with parameter b is returned.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The algorithms SVT-init and SVT-call describe a continual mechanism SVT with state ( h, ϵ, q, τ ) that maps input queries in { 0 , 1 } d to answers in {⊤ , ⊥} . In our language, whenever HSS calls SVT-init ( ϵ/ 6 , q, h ), a continual mechanism SVT ϵ/ 6 ,q,h with the single initial state ( ϵ/ 6 , q, h, τ = null) is created. Upon receiving the first input message (i.e., when τ = null), SVT ϵ/ 6 ,q,h sets null to a sample from Lap(6 /ϵ ). Then, according to the algorithm SVT-call , it updates the state and returns an output. Receiving the subsequent queries, τ = null, and SVT ϵ/ 6 ,q,h simply updates the variables and generates an output.

Then, according to the algorithm SVT-call , it updates the state and returns an output. Receiving the subsequent queries, τ = null, and SVT ϵ/ 6 ,q,h simply updates the variables and generates an output.

texts.orig: For positive integers t and j , β ∈ (0 , 1], and ϵ > 0, let γ ( t, j, β, ϵ ) and ξ ( t, j, β, ϵ ) are some deterministic functions. Given an input stream x 1 , x 2 , . . . with x i ∈ { 0 , 1 } d , the algorithm partitions the input stream into subsequences, called intervals . At the end of each interval, it gives the d -dimensional sum of all the inputs of the interval to a differentially-private histogram mechanism H . Note that H can be used directly to solve the problem; however, the new mechanism HSS has better accuracy bounds in many cases.
texts.text: For positive integers t and j , β ∈ (0 , 1], and ϵ > 0, let γ ( t, j, β, ϵ ) and ξ ( t, j, β, ϵ ) are some deterministic functions. Given an input stream x 1 , x 2 , . . . with x i ∈ { 0 , 1 } d , the algorithm partitions the input stream into subsequences, called intervals . At the end of each interval, it gives the d -dimensional sum of all the inputs of the interval to a differentially-private histogram mechanism H . Note that H can be used directly to solve the problem; however, the new mechanism HSS has better accuracy bounds in many cases.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: H is described by two operations, namely H-init ( ϵ ) and H-add ( x ), where x is a d -dimensional vector. The first initializes d empty differentially private continual binary counters with privacy parameter ϵ/d . The second operation gives each element of the d -dimensional vector x to the corresponding binary counter and returns their aggregated outputs as a d -dimensional vector. In another version of the algorithm, H-init ( ϵ, δ ) initializes d empty differentially private continual binary counters with privacy parameters ϵ/d and δ/d . In our language, H is a continual mechanism that stores the required variables in its state and upon receiving a query in { 0 , 1 } d , updates its state (i.e., the variables) and returns an answer in R d . The initial state of H is the tuple of initial values for the variables, specified by algorithm H-init .
texts.text: H is described by two operations, namely H-init ( ϵ ) and H-add ( x ), where x is a d -dimensional vector. The first initializes d empty differentially private continual binary counters with privacy parameter ϵ/d . The second operation gives each element of the d -dimensional vector x to the corresponding binary counter and returns their aggregated outputs as a d -dimensional vector. In another version of the algorithm, H-init ( ϵ, δ ) initializes d empty differentially private continual binary counters with privacy parameters ϵ/d and δ/d . In our language, H is a continual mechanism that stores the required variables in its state and upon receiving a query in { 0 , 1 } d , updates its state (i.e., the variables) and returns an answer in R d . The initial state of H is the tuple of initial values for the variables, specified by algorithm H-init .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The continual histogram query mechanism HSS keeps two d -dimensional vectors, called h and c , namely for each coordinate i ∈ { 1 , 2 , . . . , d } a variable c i and a variable h i , where c i keeps the actual sum of column i for the current interval and h i keeps the approximate sum of column i for all inputs before in the current interval as computed by H . Both are initially 0. The algorithm also keeps at any point an instance of SVT and initializes it with ϵ/ 6, q , and h . It also initializes Thresh using γ (1 , 1 , β, ϵ ) . On a new input x ( t ) the algorithm updates c and then calls SVT-call ( x ( t ) , Thresh ) using the threshold Thresh . If it returns ⊤ , the algorithm ends the current interval, inputs c to H , sets h to be the output of H , and sets c to the all-zero vector. As SVT has ended, it creates a new instance of SVT with the call SVT-init ( ϵ/ 6 , q, h ). It then applies the Laplacian mechanism to q ( c + h ) and compares its outcome with a slightly lower threshold Thresh ′ that is computed using Thresh and ξ ( t, j, β, ϵ ). If it is larger, Thresh is suitably increased. Independent of the outcome of
