texts.label: text
texts.orig: 7.2. New Privacy Analysis. Algorithm 4 describes a CM HSS with query set { 0 , 1 } d and answer set N d . The state of HSS consists of the variables ( h, c, j, out, Thresh ), initialized with (0 d , 0 d , 1 , q (0 d ) , γ (1 , 1 , β, ϵ )). The summation q ( c + h ) + Lap(3 /ϵ ) in this algorithm can be rewritten as an in instance of Laplace mechanism with privacy parameter ϵ/ 3 and input q ( c + h ). Consequently, HSS executes one instance of a histogram mechanism, H , an unbounded number of SVT instances, and an unbounded number of Laplace mechanisms. The Laplace mechanism with privacy parameter ϵ/ 3 is a NIM that is known to be ϵ/ 3-DP when receiving real values differing by at most 1. In other words, the Laplace mechanism is a CM that is ϵ/ 3-DP w.r.t. a verification function f Lap that returns ⊤ if and only if it receives a single input message m = ( m 0 , m 1 ) ∈ R 2 satisfying | m 0 -m 1 | ≤ 1.
texts.text: 7.2. New Privacy Analysis. Algorithm 4 describes a CM HSS with query set { 0 , 1 } d and answer set N d . The state of HSS consists of the variables ( h, c, j, out, Thresh ), initialized with (0 d , 0 d , 1 , q (0 d ) , γ (1 , 1 , β, ϵ )). The summation q ( c + h ) + Lap(3 /ϵ ) in this algorithm can be rewritten as an in instance of Laplace mechanism with privacy parameter ϵ/ 3 and input q ( c + h ). Consequently, HSS executes one instance of a histogram mechanism, H , an unbounded number of SVT instances, and an unbounded number of Laplace mechanisms. The Laplace mechanism with privacy parameter ϵ/ 3 is a NIM that is known to be ϵ/ 3-DP when receiving real values differing by at most 1. In other words, the Laplace mechanism is a CM that is ϵ/ 3-DP w.r.t. a verification function f Lap that returns ⊤ if and only if it receives a single input message m = ( m 0 , m 1 ) ∈ R 2 satisfying | m 0 -m 1 | ≤ 1.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The output of H is a vector in R d , while the output of the Laplace mechanism is a scalar in R . Rounding up each real-valued output to the nearest integer does not affect privacy guarantees (by the post-processing lemma) but increases the additive error of each mechanism by at most 1. This extra error is negligible compared to their original error. Therefore, we assume that both H and the Laplace mechanism have discrete answer distributions. Since the answer set of SVT is finite, it also has discrete answer distributions.
texts.text: The output of H is a vector in R d , while the output of the Laplace mechanism is a scalar in R . Rounding up each real-valued output to the nearest integer does not affect privacy guarantees (by the post-processing lemma) but increases the additive error of each mechanism by at most 1. This extra error is negligible compared to their original error. Therefore, we assume that both H and the Laplace mechanism have discrete answer distributions. Since the answer set of SVT is finite, it also has discrete answer distributions.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: By design, HSS uses the inputs x ( t ) only to determine the inputs of its sub-mechanisms, and every other action is taken solely based on the privatized outputs of the sub-mechanisms. This allows us to apply Theorem 6.1 to analyze the privacy of HSS based on the privacy parameters of its sub-mechanisms.

texts.orig: By Lemma 7.3, each instance of SVT created by HSS is ϵ/ 3-DP. Furthermore, for any two neighboring input streams, fixing the history of privatized outputs of the sub-mechanisms, HSS provides identical inputs to all but one SVT instance, which receives neighboring inputs. By Theorem 5.2, the parallel concurrent composition of all SVT instances is ϵ/ 3-DP.

texts.orig: Similarly, HSS gives identical real-valued inputs to all but one instance of the Laplace mechanism, where the differing inputs vary by at most 1. This implies that the concurrent composition of all Laplace mechanisms is also ϵ/ 3-DP. Each concurrent composition is itself a continual mechanism. Additionally, it is given that H is ϵ/ 3-DP. By basic composition, the composition of three instances of RR ϵ/ 3 , 0 is ϵ -DP. Therefore, by Theorem 4.10, the concurrent composition of all mechanismsH , SVT instances, and Laplace mechanisms-is ϵ -DP. Applying Theorem 6.1, the mechanism HSS is ϵ -DP.

texts.orig: Appendix A. Overview and Modification of [18]
texts.text: Appendix A. Overview and Modification of [18]

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this section, we overview and modify the results of [18]. The original proof relies on the existence of a constant T that upper bounds the number of queries adversaries ask; however, our modified construction does not. Moreover, an algorithmic construction in [18]'s proof needs the answer set to be finite. Using mathematical tools, we formalize their construction and weaken this assumption to having discrete answer distributions. We show:
texts.text: In this section, we overview and modify the results of [18]. The original proof relies on the existence of a constant T that upper bounds the number of queries adversaries ask; however, our modified construction does not. Moreover, an algorithmic construction in [18]'s proof needs the answer set to be finite. Using mathematical tools, we formalize their construction and weaken this assumption to having discrete answer distributions. We show:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Lemma A.1. For ϵ > 0 and 0 ≤ δ ≤ 1 , let M : S M × Q M → S M × A M be an ( ϵ, δ ) -DP IM w.r.t. a neighboring relation ∼ . Suppose that M has discrete answer distributions. Then, for any two neighboring initial states s 0 and s 1 , there exists an IPM P such that for each b ∈ { 0 , 1 } , the mechanisms M ( s b ) and P ◦ ∗ RR ϵ,δ ( b ) are identical.
texts.text: Lemma A.1. For ϵ > 0 and 0 ≤ δ ≤ 1 , let M : S M × Q M → S M × A M be an ( ϵ, δ ) -DP IM w.r.t. a neighboring relation ∼ . Suppose that M has discrete answer distributions. Then, for any two neighboring initial states s 0 and s 1 , there exists an IPM P such that for each b ∈ { 0 , 1 } , the mechanisms M ( s b ) and P ◦ ∗ RR ϵ,δ ( b ) are identical.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Notation A.2. For short, let Q = Q M and A = A M . For any T ∈ N , let [ T ] = { 1 , . . . , T } . For each t ∈ N and b ∈ { 0 , 1 } , we define the function µ b t : ( Q × A ) t → [0 , 1] such that µ b t ( q 1 , . . . , a t ) denotes the probability of M ( s b ) returning answers a 1 , . . . , a t to the queries q 1 , . . . , q t .
texts.text: Notation A.2. For short, let Q = Q M and A = A M . For any T ∈ N , let [ T ] = { 1 , . . . , T } . For each t ∈ N and b ∈ { 0 , 1 } , we define the function µ b t : ( Q × A ) t → [0 , 1] such that µ b t ( q 1 , . . . , a t ) denotes the probability of M ( s b ) returning answers a 1 , . . . , a t to the queries q 1 , . . . , q t .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Fixing an upper bound T on the number of queries, Lyu [18] reduces the construction of P to defining a sequence of functions ϕ 0 t and ϕ 1 t for each t ∈ [ T ], satisfying three conditions. They then inductively construct each ϕ b t and verify that it satisfies the required conditions. The definition of these conditions is based on a sequence of control function L 0 t,T and L 1 t,T , which have backward recursive definitions for t from T to 1.
texts.text: Fixing an upper bound T on the number of queries, Lyu [18] reduces the construction of P to defining a sequence of functions ϕ 0 t and ϕ 1 t for each t ∈ [ T ], satisfying three conditions. They then inductively construct each ϕ b t and verify that it satisfies the required conditions. The definition of these conditions is based on a sequence of control function L 0 t,T and L 1 t,T , which have backward recursive definitions for t from T to 1.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In Section A.1.1, we overview the definition of L b t,T and its properties. In Section A.1.2, we introduce a new control function L b t with no dependency on T . The goal is to use L b t instead of L b t,T in [18]'s proofs. So, in Section A.1.3, we show some properties for L b t , required in later proofs.
texts.text: In Section A.1.1, we overview the definition of L b t,T and its properties. In Section A.1.2, we introduce a new control function L b t with no dependency on T . The goal is to use L b t instead of L b t,T in [18]'s proofs. So, in Section A.1.3, we show some properties for L b t , required in later proofs.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In Section A.2, we overview the reduction presented in [18], with the control functions L b t,T replaced by L b t . In this section, we prove the reduction still holds with the new control functions. Finally, in Section A.3, we present a construction for ϕ t and show it satisfies all three conditions. We note that this construction is identical in essence to one given in [18], but we extend the proof to work with weaker assumptions.
texts.text: In Section A.2, we overview the reduction presented in [18], with the control functions L b t,T replaced by L b t . In this section, we prove the reduction still holds with the new control functions. Finally, in Section A.3, we present a construction for ϕ t and show it satisfies all three conditions. We note that this construction is identical in essence to one given in [18], but we extend the proof to work with weaker assumptions.

texts.orig: A.2. Reduction. To define P , Lyu [18] constructs four families of PMFs F (0 , ⊥ ) , F (1 , ⊥ ) , F (0 , ⊤ ) , and F (1 , ⊤ ) , corresponding to each outcome of RR ϵ,δ . Intuitively, each family describes an IM. Specifically, each family consists of PMFs over the answer set A for every t ∈ [ T ] and every history ( q 1 , . . . , a t -1 , q t ) ∈ ( Q × A ) t × Q , representing the probability of returning each answer in response to q t , conditioned on q 1 , . . . , a t -1 being the history of queries and answers.
texts.text: A.2. Reduction. To define P , Lyu [18] constructs four families of PMFs F (0 , ⊥ ) , F (1 , ⊥ ) , F (0 , ⊤ ) , and F (1 , ⊤ ) , corresponding to each outcome of RR ϵ,δ . Intuitively, each family describes an IM. Specifically, each family consists of PMFs over the answer set A for every t ∈ [ T ] and every history ( q 1 , . . . , a t -1 , q t ) ∈ ( Q × A ) t × Q , representing the probability of returning each answer in response to q t , conditioned on q 1 , . . . , a t -1 being the history of queries and answers.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: A.2.1. Constructing P using F r s. Roughly speaking, in the interaction between P ◦ ∗ RR ϵ,δ ( b ) and an adversary A , P interacts with RR ϵ,δ ( b ) once and stores its output r as thee first parameter in its state. It then answers the adversary's queries by itself using the PMFs provided by F r . Specifically, P has a single initial state (null , ()), where null is an empty field and () represents an empty sequence. Let h denote the sequence of interactions between P and A , which is stored by P as second parameter of its state. After interacting with RR ϵ,δ ( b ), P updates null to r . Moreover, upon receiving a query q and before returning an answer a , P appends q and a to the history sequence h . To generate a response a to a query q , P samples a from the PMF corresponding to ( h, q ) in F r , where h is the history of all previous queries and answers.

To generate a response a to a query q , P samples a from the PMF corresponding to ( h, q ) in F r , where h is the history of all previous queries and answers.

texts.orig: A.2.2. Constructing F ( b, ⊥ ) s. Lyu [18] constructs a function ϕ t : ( Q × A ) t → [0 , 1] 2 for each t ∈ [ T ] ∪ { 0 } . Let ϕ 0 t and ϕ 1 t denote the functions returning the first and second elements of the output pairs of ϕ t . For each b ∈ { 0 , 1 } , the family F ( b, ⊥ ) is defined based on ϕ b t s. Intuitively, ϕ b t ( q 1 , . . . , a t ) represents the probability that M ( s b ) returns the answers a 1 , . . . , a t to the fixed queries q 1 , . . . , q t . That is, for each query sequence q 1 , . . . , q t , ϕ b t ( q 1 , · , q 2 , · , . . . , q t , · ) is a PMF over A t .

texts.orig: Roughly speaking, Lyu [18] initializes the values of ϕ t with the ones from L t . Then, for each q 1 , . . . , a t -1 , q t , they iterate over all possible answers a t ∈ A in a fixed order and gradually increase the value of ϕ t ( q 1 , . . . , a t ) to satisfy conditions (II) and (III), while ensuring that condition (I) remains valid. This iterative procedure requires A to be finite; otherwise, the construction algorithm does not terminate. To extend this approach to infinite A , we define ϕ t mathematically and apply the transfinite recursion theorem to prove its existence.

texts.orig: Definition A.23 (Well-Order) . A well-order on a set X is a total order (i.e., for every x, y ∈ X , either x < y , x > y , or x = y ) such that every non-empty subset of X has a least element.
texts.text: Definition A.23 (Well-Order) . A well-order on a set X is a total order (i.e., for every x, y ∈ X , either x < y , x > y , or x = y ) such that every non-empty subset of X has a least element.

texts.orig: Since A is countable, we can inject A into N . By listing elements of A in the order given by their images under this injection, we get a well-order < A on A . We will later use the following remark when constructing ξ t and ϕ t .

texts.orig: Lemma A.26 (Transfinite Recursion) . Let f be a function recursively defined over ( Q × A ) t according to the well-order < t . If for each sequence ( q 1 , . . . , a t ) ∈ ( Q × A ) t , the value of f ( q 1 , . . . , a t ) is uniquely determined by the values f ( q ′ 1 , . . . , a ′ t ) for every ( q ′ 1 , . . . , a ′ t ) < t ( q 1 , . . . , a t ) , then f exists and is unique.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For ϵ ≥ 0, let M : S M × Q M → S M × A M be an ϵ -DP IM w.r.t. a neighboring relation ∼ . Suppose that M has discrete answer distributions. Vadhan and Wang [20] shows that for any two neighboring initial states s 0 and s 1 , there exists an IPM P such that for each b ∈ { 0 , 1 } , the mechanisms M ( s b ) and P ◦ ∗ RR ϵ, 0 ( b ) are identical. In this section, we overview the construction of P and show it satisfies the following property:
texts.text: For ϵ ≥ 0, let M : S M × Q M → S M × A M be an ϵ -DP IM w.r.t. a neighboring relation ∼ . Suppose that M has discrete answer distributions. Vadhan and Wang [20] shows that for any two neighboring initial states s 0 and s 1 , there exists an IPM P such that for each b ∈ { 0 , 1 } , the mechanisms M ( s b ) and P ◦ ∗ RR ϵ, 0 ( b ) are identical. In this section, we overview the construction of P and show it satisfies the following property:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Claim B.1. For any t ∈ N and any query sequence ( q 1 , . . . , q t ) ∈ Q t M , if the distributions of the first t answers of M ( s 0 ) and M ( s 1 ) to the queries ( q 1 , . . . , q t ) are identical, then for every b ∈ { 0 , 1 } , when P ◦ ∗ RR ϵ, 0 ( b ) receives the queries q 1 , . . . , q t , P generates answers itself, without interacting with RR ϵ, 0 ( b ) .

texts.orig: Vadhan and Wang [20] define P as follows: The initial state of P is (null , ()), where null is an empty field and () represents an empty sequence. Let h denote the sequence. Let ( r, ⊥ ) denote the outcome of RR ϵ, 0 ( b ). After interacting with RR ϵ, 0 ( b ), P updates null to r . Moreover, upon receiving a query q and before returning an answer a , P appends q and a to the history sequence h . To generate a response a to a query q , P samples a from the distribution T r h,q , where h is the history of all previous queries and answers.
texts.text: Vadhan and Wang [20] define P as follows: The initial state of P is (null , ()), where null is an empty field and () represents an empty sequence. Let h denote the sequence. Let ( r, ⊥ ) denote the outcome of RR ϵ, 0 ( b ). After interacting with RR ϵ, 0 ( b ), P updates null to r . Moreover, upon receiving a query q and before returning an answer a , P appends q and a to the history sequence h . To generate a response a to a query q , P samples a from the distribution T r h,q , where h is the history of all previous queries and answers.

texts.orig: Proof of Claim B.1. That the distributions µ M ( s 0 ) q 1 ,...,q t and µ M ( s 1 ) q 1 ,...,q t are identical implies that for every k ≤ t , µ M ( s 0 ) q 1 ,...,q k and µ M ( s 1 ) q 1 ,...,q k are identical. Consequently, by definition, both distributions ν 0 q 1 ,...,q k and ν 1 q 1 ,...,q k are identical to µ 0 q 1 ,...,q k . Therefore, the conditional distributions T 0 q 1 ,...,a t -1 ,q t and T 1 q 1 ,...,a t -1 ,q t used by P are also identical, implying that P does not interact with RR ϵ, 0 to generate the first t answers. □
